subject: pgsql: Make truncate_useless_pathkeys() consider WindowFuncs
author: David Rowley <drowley(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Make truncate_useless_pathkeys() consider WindowFuncs
truncate_useless_pathkeys() seems to have neglected to account forPathKeys that might be useful for WindowClause evaluation.  Modify it sothat it properly accounts for that.
Making this work required adjusting two things:
1. Change from checking query_pathkeys to check sort_pathkeys instead.2. Add explicit check for window_pathkeys
For #1, query_pathkeys gets set in standard_qp_callback() according to thesort order requirements for the first operation to be applied after thejoin planner is finished, so this changes depending on which upperplanner operations a particular query needs.  If the query has windowfunctions and no GROUP BY, then query_pathkeys gets set towindow_pathkeys.  Before this change, this meant PathKeys useful for theORDER BY were not accounted for in queries with window functions.
Because of #1, #2 is now required so that we explicitly check to ensurewe don't truncate away PathKeys useful for window functions.
Author: David Rowley <dgrowleyml(at)gmail(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAApHDvrj3HTKmXoLMbUjTO=_MNMxM=cnuCSyBKidAVibmYPnrg@mail.gmail.com

before_code: * Because we the have the possibility of incremental sort, a prefix list of
* keys is potentially useful for improving the performance of the requested
* ordering. Thus we return 0, if no valuable keys are found, or the number
* of leading keys shared by the list and the requested ordering..
after_code: * Because we the have the possibility of incremental sort, a prefix list of
* keys is potentially useful for improving the performance of the requested
* ordering. Thus we return 0, if no valuable keys are found, or the number
* of leading keys shared by the list and the requested ordering.
*     Count the number of pathkeys that are useful for meeting the
*     query's desired sort order for window function evaluation.

removed: * of leading keys shared by the list and the requested ordering..
added: * of leading keys shared by the list and the requested ordering.
*     Count the number of pathkeys that are useful for meeting the
*     query's desired sort order for window function evaluation.

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test that pathkeys useful for window functions are preserved when there are no GROUP BY clauses. This verifies the new pathkeys_useful_for_windowing function and the change from query_pathkeys to sort_pathkeys.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_window_t1 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_window_t1 (a INT, b INT, c INT);
INSERT INTO test_window_t1 SELECT i, i % 10, i % 5 FROM generate_series(1, 100) i;
ANALYZE test_window_t1;

-- Execution: Window function with ORDER BY that matches potential index order
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT a, b, SUM(c) OVER (ORDER BY a, b) FROM test_window_t1 ORDER BY a, b;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_window_t1 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test that pathkeys for window functions are considered even when there is also a plain ORDER BY at query level. This ensures both sort_pathkeys and window_pathkeys are evaluated.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_window_order CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_window_order (x INT, y INT, z INT);
INSERT INTO test_window_order SELECT i, i % 7, i % 3 FROM generate_series(1, 50) i;
ANALYZE test_window_order;

-- Execution: Query with window function ordering and outer ORDER BY
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT x, y, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY x, y) FROM test_window_order ORDER BY x, y, z;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_window_order CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test that pathkeys useful for merging (joins) are still preserved when window functions are present. This verifies the max(nuseful, nuseful2) logic works correctly.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_join_window_t1 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_join_window_t2 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_join_window_t1 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
CREATE TABLE test_join_window_t2 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
INSERT INTO test_join_window_t1 SELECT i, i % 20 FROM generate_series(1, 100) i;
INSERT INTO test_join_window_t2 SELECT i, i % 30 FROM generate_series(1, 100) i;
ANALYZE test_join_window_t1, test_join_window_t2;

-- Execution: Join with merge join possibility and window function
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT t1.id, t2.val, SUM(t1.val) OVER (ORDER BY t1.id)
FROM test_join_window_t1 t1 JOIN test_join_window_t2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id
ORDER BY t1.id;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_join_window_t2 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_join_window_t1 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test that pathkeys for grouping are still considered when window functions are present. This ensures the grouping check happens after windowing check.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_group_window CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_group_window (grp INT, val INT, extra INT);
INSERT INTO test_group_window SELECT i % 5, i, i % 3 FROM generate_series(1, 100) i;
ANALYZE test_group_window;

-- Execution: Query with GROUP BY and window function with different order
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT grp, SUM(val), AVG(val) OVER (ORDER BY extra)
FROM test_group_window
GROUP BY grp, extra
ORDER BY grp, extra;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_group_window CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test boundary case with no useful pathkeys for ordering, merging, windowing, or grouping. This ensures truncation logic works when all are zero.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_no_useful CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_no_useful (a INT, b INT);
INSERT INTO test_no_useful SELECT i, i*2 FROM generate_series(1, 10) i;
ANALYZE test_no_useful;

-- Execution: Simple scan with window function but no matching pathkeys
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT a, b, COUNT(*) OVER ()
FROM test_no_useful
WHERE a > 5;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_no_useful CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Calculate agglevelsup correctly when Aggref contains a CTE.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Calculate agglevelsup correctly when Aggref contains a CTE.
If an aggregate function call contains a sub-select that hasan RTE referencing a CTE outside the aggregate, we must treatthat reference like a Var referencing the CTE's query levelfor purposes of determining the aggregate's level.  Otherwisewe might reach the nonsensical conclusion that the aggregateshould be evaluated at some query level higher than the CTE,ending in a planner error or a broken plan tree that causesexecutor failures.
Bug: #19055Reported-by: BugForge <dllggyx(at)outlook(dot)com>Author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/19055-6970cfa8556a394d@postgresql.orgBackpatch-through: 13

after_code: * CTE references act similarly to Vars of the CTE's level.  Without
* this we might conclude that the Agg can be evaluated above the CTE,
/* convert levelsup to frame of reference of original query */
return false;           /* allow range_table_walker to continue */

added: * CTE references act similarly to Vars of the CTE's level.  Without
* this we might conclude that the Agg can be evaluated above the CTE,
/* convert levelsup to frame of reference of original query */
return false;           /* allow range_table_walker to continue */

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test aggregate with CTE reference inside sub-select, ensuring agglevelsup is calculated correctly to avoid planner error.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t1 (a INT);
INSERT INTO test_t1 VALUES (1), (2), (3);

-- Execution
WITH cte AS (SELECT a FROM test_t1)
SELECT (SELECT sum(a) FROM (SELECT a FROM cte WHERE cte.a = test_t1.a) s) FROM test_t1;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test aggregate containing a CTE reference at a higher query level, verifying correct level calculation across nested subqueries.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t2 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t2 (x INT);
INSERT INTO test_t2 VALUES (10), (20);

-- Execution
WITH cte AS (SELECT x FROM test_t2)
SELECT (SELECT (SELECT sum(x) FROM (SELECT x FROM cte WHERE cte.x = outer_sub.x) ss) FROM (SELECT x FROM test_t2) outer_sub) FROM test_t2 LIMIT 1;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t2 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test multiple CTEs with aggregates in sub-selects, ensuring each CTE reference is handled properly without executor failures.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t3 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t3 (val INT);
INSERT INTO test_t3 VALUES (5), (15), (25);

-- Execution
WITH cte1 AS (SELECT val FROM test_t3),
     cte2 AS (SELECT val FROM cte1 WHERE val > 10)
SELECT (SELECT avg(val) FROM (SELECT val FROM cte2 WHERE cte2.val = test_t3.val) sub) FROM test_t3;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t3 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test aggregate with CTE reference in a HAVING clause subquery, validating agglevelsup calculation in complex filter contexts.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t4 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t4 (id INT, grp INT);
INSERT INTO test_t4 VALUES (1, 1), (2, 1), (3, 2), (4, 2);

-- Execution
WITH cte AS (SELECT grp FROM test_t4)
SELECT grp, sum(id) FROM test_t4 GROUP BY grp
HAVING sum(id) > (SELECT min(grp) FROM cte WHERE cte.grp = test_t4.grp);

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t4 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test nested CTEs with aggregate in a sub-select that references outer CTE, ensuring correct level handling to prevent broken plan trees.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t5 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t5 (num INT);
INSERT INTO test_t5 VALUES (100), (200), (300);

-- Execution
WITH outer_cte AS (
    SELECT num FROM test_t5
),
inner_cte AS (
    SELECT num FROM outer_cte
)
SELECT (SELECT count(num) FROM (SELECT num FROM inner_cte WHERE inner_cte.num = outer_cte.num) s) FROM outer_cte;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t5 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Add missing EPQ recheck for TID Scan
author: David Rowley <drowley(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Add missing EPQ recheck for TID Scan
The EvalPlanQual recheck for TID Scan wasn't rechecking the TID qualstill passed after following update chains.  This could result in tuplesbeing updated or deleted by plans using TID Scans where the ctid of thenew (updated) tuple no longer matches the clause of the scan.  This isn'tdesired behavior, and isn't consistent with what would happen if thechosen plan had used an Index or Seq Scan, and that could lead to hard topredict behavior for scans that contain TID quals and other quals as theplanner has freedom to choose TID or some other scan method for suchqueries, and the chosen plan could change at any moment.
Here we fix this by properly implementing the recheck function for TIDScans.
Backpatch to 13, oldest supported version
Reported-by: Sophie Alpert <pg(at)sophiebits(dot)com>Author: Sophie Alpert <pg(at)sophiebits(dot)com>Author: David Rowley <dgrowleyml(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: David Rowley <dgrowleyml(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Chao Li <li(dot)evan(dot)chao(at)gmail(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/4a6268ff-3340-453a-9bf5-c98d51a6f729@app.fastmail.comBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: * XXX shouldn't we check here to make sure tuple matches TID list? In
* runtime-key case this is not certain, is it?  However, in the WHERE
* CURRENT OF case it might not match anyway ...
after_code: /* WHERE CURRENT OF always intends to resolve to the latest tuple */
* Binary search the TidList to see if this ctid is mentioned and return

removed: * XXX shouldn't we check here to make sure tuple matches TID list? In
* runtime-key case this is not certain, is it?  However, in the WHERE
* CURRENT OF case it might not match anyway ...
added: /* WHERE CURRENT OF always intends to resolve to the latest tuple */
* Binary search the TidList to see if this ctid is mentioned and return

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test EPQ recheck for TID Scan with UPDATE conflict where ctid changes after update chain</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_tid_epq CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_tid_epq (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
INSERT INTO test_tid_epq VALUES (1, 100), (2, 200);
BEGIN;
-- Session A: Lock row to create conflict
SELECT * FROM test_tid_epq WHERE id = 1 FOR UPDATE;
-- Session B (simulated in same transaction with savepoints):
SAVEPOINT s1;
UPDATE test_tid_epq SET val = 101 WHERE id = 1;
-- This TID scan should recheck ctid after EPQ
SELECT * FROM test_tid_epq WHERE ctid = '(0,1)' FOR UPDATE NOWAIT;
ROLLBACK TO s1;
-- Cleanup
ROLLBACK;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_tid_epq CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test TID Scan recheck with runtime key where TID list changes during execution</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_tid_runtime CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_tid_runtime (id INT);
INSERT INTO test_tid_runtime SELECT generate_series(1, 1000);
-- Create function to generate TID list
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_tids() RETURNS tid[] AS $$
DECLARE
    tids tid[];
BEGIN
    SELECT array_agg(ctid) INTO tids FROM test_tid_runtime WHERE id % 2 = 0;
    RETURN tids;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql STABLE;
-- Test with changing TID list
BEGIN;
-- Force TID scan with runtime parameter
PREPARE tid_query(tid[]) AS SELECT * FROM test_tid_runtime WHERE ctid = ANY($1);
EXECUTE tid_query(get_tids());
-- Modify data to change ctids
DELETE FROM test_tid_runtime WHERE id % 4 = 0;
-- Re-execute should recheck with updated TID list
EXECUTE tid_query(get_tids());
COMMIT;
-- Cleanup
DROP FUNCTION get_tids();
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_tid_runtime CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test WHERE CURRENT OF with TID Scan to verify it bypasses recheck</description>
<sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_current_of CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_current_of (id INT PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_current_of VALUES (1, 'initial');
BEGIN;
-- Declare cursor with WHERE CURRENT OF
DECLARE cur CURSOR FOR SELECT * FROM test_current_of FOR UPDATE;
FETCH cur;
-- Update the tuple (changes ctid)
UPDATE test_current_of SET data = 'updated' WHERE CURRENT OF cur;
-- Verify cursor still works with WHERE CURRENT OF
UPDATE test_current_of SET data = 'final' WHERE CURRENT OF cur;
COMMIT;
-- Verify final state
SELECT * FROM test_current_of;
-- Cleanup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_current_of CASCADE;
</sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test TID Scan with multiple quals including non-TID conditions</description>
<sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_mixed_quals CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_mixed_quals (id INT, flag BOOLEAN, data TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_mixed_quals 
SELECT i, i % 2 = 0, 'data_' || i 
FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
-- Create index to give planner choice
CREATE INDEX ON test_mixed_quals (flag);
ANALYZE test_mixed_quals;
-- Force TID scan with ctid qual and additional filter
BEGIN;
-- Lock some rows to create EPQ scenario
SELECT * FROM test_mixed_quals WHERE id = 500 FOR UPDATE;
-- In another session (simulated):
SAVEPOINT s1;
UPDATE test_mixed_quals SET data = 'modified' WHERE id = 500;
-- This query uses both ctid and flag qualifiers
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) 
SELECT * FROM test_mixed_quals 
WHERE ctid = '(0,499)' AND flag = true;
SELECT * FROM test_mixed_quals 
WHERE ctid = '(0,499)' AND flag = true FOR UPDATE NOWAIT;
ROLLBACK TO s1;
COMMIT;
-- Cleanup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_mixed_quals CASCADE;
</sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test concurrent UPDATE/DELETE with TID Scan to verify recheck prevents wrong tuple modification</description>
<sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_tid CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_concurrent_tid (id INT PRIMARY KEY, version INT);
INSERT INTO test_concurrent_tid VALUES (1, 1), (2, 1);
-- Session A: Start transaction and lock row
BEGIN;
SELECT * FROM test_concurrent_tid WHERE id = 1 FOR UPDATE;
-- Session B (simulated with savepoints):
SAVEPOINT s1;
-- This UPDATE changes ctid of tuple 1
UPDATE test_concurrent_tid SET version = 2 WHERE id = 1;
-- Attempt to modify using TID scan on old ctid
-- Should fail or skip due to recheck
SAVEPOINT s2;
DELETE FROM test_concurrent_tid WHERE ctid = '(0,1)';
ROLLBACK TO s2;
-- Verify tuple still exists with new version
SELECT * FROM test_concurrent_tid WHERE id = 1;
ROLLBACK TO s1;
-- Session A commit
COMMIT;
-- Final verification
SELECT * FROM test_concurrent_tid ORDER BY id;
-- Cleanup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_tid CASCADE;
</sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Amend recent fix for SIMILAR TO regex conversion.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Amend recent fix for SIMILAR TO regex conversion.
Commit e3ffc3e91 fixed the translation of character classes inSIMILAR TO regular expressions.  Unfortunately the fix broke a cornercase: if there is an escape character right after the opening bracket(for example in "[\q]"), a closing bracket right after the escapesequence would not be seen as closing the character class.
There were two more oversights: a backslash or a nested opening bracketright at the beginning of a character class should remove the specialmeaning from any following caret or closing bracket.
This bug suggests that this code needs to be more readable, so alsorename the variables "charclass_depth" and "charclass_start" tosomething more meaningful, rewrite an "if" cascade to be moreconsistent, and improve the commentary.
Reported-by: Dominique Devienne <ddevienne(at)gmail(dot)com>Reported-by: Stephan Springl <springl-psql(at)bfw-online(dot)de>Author: Laurenz Albe <laurenz(dot)albe(at)cybertec(dot)at>Reviewed-by: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAFCRh-8NwJd0jq6P=R3qhHyqU7hw0BTor3W0SvUcii24et+zAw@mail.gmail.comBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: int         charclass_depth = 0;    /* Nesting level of character classes,
int         charclass_start = 0;    /* State of the character class start,
* the relevant part separators in the above expansion.  If the result
* of this function is used in a plain regexp match (SIMILAR TO), the
* escape-double-quotes have no effect on the match behavior.
/* emit appropriate part separator, per notes above */
/* SQL escape character; do not send to output */
* Ignore a closing bracket at the start of a character class.
* Such a bracket is taken literally rather than closing the
* class.  "charclass_start" is 1 right at the beginning of a
* class and 2 after an initial caret.
* If there is a caret right after the opening bracket, it negates
* the character class, but a following closing bracket should
* still be treated as a normal character.  That holds only for
* the first caret, so only the values 1 and 2 mean that closing
after_code: int         bracket_depth = 0;  /* square bracket nesting level */
int         charclass_pos = 0;  /* position inside a character class */
* the relevant part separators in the above expansion.  If the result
* of this function is used in a plain regexp match (SIMILAR TO), the
* escape-double-quotes have no effect on the match behavior.
* While we don't fully validate character classes (bracket expressions),
* we do need to parse them well enough to know where they end.
* "charclass_pos" tracks where we are in a character class.
* Its value is uninteresting when bracket_depth is 0.
* But when bracket_depth > 0, it will be
*   1: right after the opening '[' (a following '^' will negate
*      the class, while ']' is a literal character)
*   2: right after a '^' after the opening '[' (']' is still a literal
*   3 or more: further inside the character class (']' ends the class)
/* emit appropriate part separator, per notes above */
* If we encounter an escaped character in a character class,
* we are no longer at the beginning.

* If we're here, backslash is not the SQL escape character,
* so treat it as a literal class element, which requires
* doubling it.  (This matches our behavior for backslashes
/* parse the character class well enough to identify ending ']' */
/* found the real end of a bracket pair */
/* don't reset charclass_pos, this may be an inner bracket */
/* start of a nested bracket pair */
* We are no longer at the beginning of a character class.
* (The nested bracket pair is a collating element, not a
* character class in its own right.)

* ']' is still a literal character and does not end the
* character class.  If we are further inside a character
* class, charclass_pos might get incremented past 3, which is
* Anything else (including a backslash or leading ']') is an
* element of the character class, so we are no longer at the

* If we encounter an escaped character in a character class,
* we are no longer at the beginning.

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test escape character right after opening bracket in character class (e.g., "[\q]") to ensure closing bracket is correctly recognized.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_escape CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_similar_escape (pattern TEXT, test_string TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_similar_escape VALUES 
('[\q]', 'q'),
('[\q]', ']'),
('[\q]', '[');

-- Execution: Should match 'q' only, not ']' or '['
SELECT pattern, test_string, 
       test_string SIMILAR TO pattern AS matches 
FROM test_similar_escape 
ORDER BY pattern, test_string;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_escape CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test backslash at beginning of character class removes special meaning of following caret or closing bracket.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_backslash CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_similar_backslash (pattern TEXT, test_string TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_similar_backslash VALUES 
('[\\^]', '^'),
('[\\^]', 'a'),
('[\\]]', ']'),
('[\\]]', 'a');

-- Execution: Should match '^' and ']' respectively, not 'a'
SELECT pattern, test_string, 
       test_string SIMILAR TO pattern AS matches 
FROM test_similar_backslash 
ORDER BY pattern, test_string;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_backslash CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test nested opening bracket at beginning of character class removes special meaning of following caret or closing bracket.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_nested CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_similar_nested (pattern TEXT, test_string TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_similar_nested VALUES 
('[[]', '['),
('[[]', ']'),
('[[]', 'a'),
('[[^]', '^'),
('[[^]', ']'),
('[[^]', 'a');

-- Execution: Should match '[' and '^' respectively, not others
SELECT pattern, test_string, 
       test_string SIMILAR TO pattern AS matches 
FROM test_similar_nested 
ORDER BY pattern, test_string;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_nested CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test character class with caret negation and escaped characters inside.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_caret CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_similar_caret (pattern TEXT, test_string TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_similar_caret VALUES 
('[^\\]]', 'a'),
('[^\\]]', ']'),
('[^\\]]', 'b'),
('[^a]', 'b'),
('[^a]', 'a');

-- Execution: First pattern should match 'a' and 'b', not ']'. Second should match 'b', not 'a'.
SELECT pattern, test_string, 
       test_string SIMILAR TO pattern AS matches 
FROM test_similar_caret 
ORDER BY pattern, test_string;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_caret CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test complex nested character classes with multiple brackets and escapes.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_complex CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_similar_complex (pattern TEXT, test_string TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_similar_complex VALUES 
('[[\\]a]', ']'),
('[[\\]a]', 'a'),
('[[\\]a]', 'b'),
('[a[\\]]', ']'),
('[a[\\]]', 'a'),
('[a[\\]]', 'b'),
('[\\[\\]]', '['),
('[\\[\\]]', ']'),
('[\\[\\]]', 'a');

-- Execution: Verify correct matching for nested and escaped brackets
SELECT pattern, test_string, 
       test_string SIMILAR TO pattern AS matches 
FROM test_similar_complex 
ORDER BY pattern, test_string;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_similar_complex CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * While we don't fully validate character classes (bracket expressions),
* we do need to parse them well enough to know where they end.
* "charclass_pos" tracks where we are in a character class.
* Its value is uninteresting when bracket_depth is 0.
* But when bracket_depth > 0, it will be
*   1: right after the opening '[' (a following '^' will negate
*      the class, while ']' is a literal character)
*   2: right after a '^' after the opening '[' (']' is still a literal
*   3 or more: further inside the character class (']' ends the class)

subject: pgsql: Fix memory leakage in nodeSubplan.c.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix memory leakage in nodeSubplan.c.
If the hash functions used for hashing tuples leaked any memory,we failed to clean that up, resulting in query-lifespan memoryleakage in queries using hashed subplans.  One way that couldhappen is if the values being hashed require de-toasting, sincemost of our hash functions don't trouble to clean up de-toastedinputs.
Prior to commit bf6c614a2, this leakage was largely maskedbecause TupleHashTableMatch would reset hashtable->tempcxt(via execTuplesMatch).  But it doesn't do that anymore, andthat's not really the right place for this anyway: doing itthere could reset the tempcxt many times per hash lookup,or not at all.  Instead put reset calls into ExecHashSubPlanand buildSubPlanHash.  Along the way to that, rearrangeExecHashSubPlan so that there's just one place to callMemoryContextReset instead of several.
This amounts to accepting the de-facto API spec that the callerof the TupleHashTable routines is responsible for resetting thetempcxt adequately often.  Although the other callers seem toget this right, it was not documented anywhere, so add a commentabout it.
Bug: #19040Reported-by: Haiyang Li <mohen(dot)lhy(at)alibaba-inc(dot)com>Author: Haiyang Li <mohen(dot)lhy(at)alibaba-inc(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Fei Changhong <feichanghong(at)qq(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/19040-c9b6073ef814f48c@postgresql.orgBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: * Note that keyColIdx, eqfunctions, and hashfunctions must be allocated in
* storage that will live as long as the hashtable does.
after_code: * Note that keyColIdx, eqfunctions, and hashfunctions must be allocated in
* storage that will live as long as the hashtable does.
* LookupTupleHashEntry, FindTupleHashEntry, and related functions may leak
* memory in the tempcxt.  It is caller's responsibility to reset that context
* reasonably often, typically once per tuple.  (We do it that way, rather
* than managing an extra context within the hashtable, because in many cases
* the caller can specify a tempcxt that it needs to reset per-tuple anyway.)
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/executor/execGrouping.c b/src/backend/executor/execGrouping.c

index 01f6d2001e12dfb9003deb82de2f82becd90d003..55f78204403282928b097195b2f46d07ab780898 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/executor/execGrouping.c
+++ b/src/backend/executor/execGrouping.c
@@ -149,6 +149,12 @@ execTuplesHashPrepare(int numCols,
  *
  * Note that keyColIdx, eqfunctions, and hashfunctions must be allocated in
  * storage that will live as long as the hashtable does.
+ *
+ * LookupTupleHashEntry, FindTupleHashEntry, and related functions may leak
+ * memory in the tempcxt.  It is caller's responsibility to reset that context
+ * reasonably often, typically once per tuple.  (We do it that way, rather
+ * than managing an extra context within the hashtable, because in many cases
+ * the caller can specify a tempcxt that it needs to reset per-tuple anyway.)
  */
 TupleHashTable
 BuildTupleHashTableExt(PlanState *parent,

added: * LookupTupleHashEntry, FindTupleHashEntry, and related functions may leak
* memory in the tempcxt.  It is caller's responsibility to reset that context
* reasonably often, typically once per tuple.  (We do it that way, rather
* than managing an extra context within the hashtable, because in many cases
* the caller can specify a tempcxt that it needs to reset per-tuple anyway.)

        <description>Test memory leak fix with hashed subplan using toasted text values that require detoasting during hash computation.</description>

-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_subplan_toast CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_main_table CASCADE;

CREATE TABLE test_subplan_toast (large_text TEXT);
CREATE TABLE test_main_table (id INT, search_text TEXT);

-- Insert toasted values (text > 2KB triggers TOAST)
INSERT INTO test_subplan_toast SELECT repeat('x', 3000) || g FROM generate_series(1, 10) g;
INSERT INTO test_main_table SELECT g, repeat('x', 3000) || g FROM generate_series(1, 5) g;

-- Execution: Hashed subplan with toasted values
SELECT * FROM test_main_table m 
WHERE search_text IN (SELECT large_text FROM test_subplan_toast);

-- Teardown

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_subplan_toast CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test memory reset in hashed subplan with multiple hash lookups per tuple using correlated subquery.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_inner CASCADE;

CREATE TABLE test_outer (val1 INT, val2 INT);
CREATE TABLE test_inner (key1 INT, key2 INT, data TEXT);

INSERT INTO test_outer SELECT g, g*10 FROM generate_series(1, 100) g;
INSERT INTO test_inner SELECT g, g*10, repeat('detoast_me', 500) FROM generate_series(1, 50) g;

-- Execution: Correlated subquery with hashed execution
SELECT * FROM test_outer o 
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM test_inner i WHERE i.key1 = o.val1 AND i.key2 = o.val2);

-- Teardown

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_inner CASCADE;
        </sql>

CREATE TABLE test_notin_inner (exclude_id INT, exclude_text TEXT);

INSERT INTO test_notin_outer SELECT g, repeat('toasted_value_', 200) || g FROM generate_series(1, 100) g;
INSERT INTO test_notin_inner VALUES (1, NULL), (2, NULL), (3, repeat('long_toast', 300));

-- Execution: NOT IN with hashed subplan (handles nulls specially)

WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT exclude_id FROM test_notin_inner);

    </test_case>

CREATE TABLE test_array_inner (arr INT[]);

INSERT INTO test_array_inner SELECT array_agg(g) FROM generate_series(1, 500) g;

INSERT INTO test_array_outer SELECT array_agg(g) FROM generate_series(1, 500) g;

-- Execution: Hashed subplan with array hashing (uses hash_array which may detoast)

WHERE arr IN (SELECT arr FROM test_array_inner);

-- Teardown

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_array_inner CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test memory leakage fix with multiple sequential hashed subqueries in single query to verify per-tuple reset.</description>
       <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi_subplan1 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi_subplan2 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi_main CASCADE;

CREATE TABLE test_multi_subplan1 (t TEXT);
CREATE TABLE test_multi_subplan2 (t TEXT);
CREATE TABLE test_multi_main (t TEXT);

INSERT INTO test_multi_subplan1 SELECT repeat('subplan1_', 300) || g FROM generate_series(1, 20) g;
INSERT INTO test_multi_subplan2 SELECT repeat('subplan2_', 300) || g FROM generate_series(1, 20) g;
INSERT INTO test_multi_main SELECT repeat('main_', 300) || g FROM generate_series(1, 30) g;

-- Execution: Multiple hashed subplans in OR condition
SELECT * FROM test_multi_main 
WHERE t IN (SELECT t FROM test_multi_subplan1)
   OR t IN (SELECT t FROM test_multi_subplan2);

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi_main CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi_subplan1 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE test_multi_subplan2 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * LookupTupleHashEntry, FindTupleHashEntry, and related functions may leak
* memory in the tempcxt.  It is caller's responsibility to reset that context
* reasonably often, typically once per tuple.  (We do it that way, rather
* than managing an extra context within the hashtable, because in many cases
* the caller can specify a tempcxt that it needs to reset per-tuple anyway.)

subject: pgsql: Revert "Get rid of WALBufMappingLock"
author: Alexander Korotkov <akorotkov(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Revert "Get rid of WALBufMappingLock"
This reverts commit bc22dc0e0ddc2dcb6043a732415019cc6b6bf683.It appears that conditional variables are not suitable for use insidecritical sections.  If WaitLatch()/WaitEventSetWaitBlock() face postmasterdeath, they exit, releasing all locks instead of PANIC.  In certainsituations, this leads to data corruption.

<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test concurrent WAL buffer initialization under heavy insert load to verify WALBufMappingLock serialization.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_wal CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_concurrent_wal (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
CREATE INDEX idx_test_concurrent_wal ON test_concurrent_wal(data);

-- Execution: Generate concurrent inserts to stress WAL buffer mapping
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_concurrent_wal (data) SELECT 'test' || g FROM generate_series(1, 1000) g;
COMMIT;

-- Verify data integrity
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_concurrent_wal;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_wal CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test WAL buffer page replacement during checkpoint with full_page_writes to verify lock handling.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_checkpoint_wal CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_checkpoint_wal (id INT, payload TEXT);
ALTER SYSTEM SET full_page_writes = on;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

-- Execution: Force checkpoint and concurrent inserts
CHECKPOINT;
INSERT INTO test_checkpoint_wal SELECT g, repeat('x', 1000) FROM generate_series(1, 500) g;
CHECKPOINT;

-- Verify
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_checkpoint_wal;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_checkpoint_wal CASCADE;
ALTER SYSTEM SET full_page_writes = DEFAULT;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test WAL buffer initialization boundary when crossing WAL segment to verify InitializedUpTo updates.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_segment CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_wal_segment (id INT, data TEXT);
-- Force new WAL segment
SELECT pg_switch_wal();

-- Execution: Insert enough data to cross segment boundary
INSERT INTO test_wal_segment SELECT g, repeat('data', 100) FROM generate_series(1, 10000) g;

-- Verify by checking WAL position advancement
SELECT pg_current_wal_lsn() > '0/0' AS wal_advanced;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_segment CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test opportunistic WAL buffer pre-initialization by WAL writer under low load.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_opportunistic_wal CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_opportunistic_wal (id INT);

-- Execution: Minimal insert to trigger WAL writer pre-initialization
INSERT INTO test_opportunistic_wal VALUES (1);
-- Allow WAL writer to run
SELECT pg_sleep(0.1);

-- Verify
SELECT * FROM test_opportunistic_wal;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_opportunistic_wal CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test recovery initialization path with WALBufMappingLock after crash-like scenario.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create table and force checkpoint
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_wal CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_recovery_wal (id INT PRIMARY KEY);
INSERT INTO test_recovery_wal VALUES (1);
CHECKPOINT;

-- Execution: Simulate recovery-like WAL buffer initialization
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_recovery_wal VALUES (2);
-- Force WAL flush
SELECT pg_current_wal_flush_lsn();
COMMIT;

-- Verify recovery consistency
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_recovery_wal;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_wal CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * WALBufMappingLock: must be held to replace a page in the WAL buffer cache.
* It is only held while initializing and changing the mapping.  If the
* contents of the buffer being replaced haven't been written yet, the mapping
* lock is released while the write is done, and reacquired afterwards.
* To change the identity of a buffer (and InitializedUpTo), you need to
* hold WALBufMappingLock.  To change the identity of a buffer that's
* page is not initialized yet, you have to grab WALBufMappingLock to
* initialize it, but the WAL writer tries to do that ahead of insertions
* to avoid that from happening in the critical path.

subject: pgsql: Fix self-deadlock during DROP SUBSCRIPTION.
author: Amit Kapila <akapila(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix self-deadlock during DROP SUBSCRIPTION.
The DROP SUBSCRIPTION command performs several operations: it stops thesubscription workers, removes subscription-related entries from systemcatalogs, and deletes the replication slot on the publisher server.Previously, this command acquired an AccessExclusiveLock onpg_subscription before initiating these steps.
However, while holding this lock, the command attempts to connect to thepublisher to remove the replication slot. In cases where the connection ismade to a newly created database on the same server as subscriber, thecache-building process during connection tries to acquire anAccessShareLock on pg_subscription, resulting in a self-deadlock.
To resolve this issue, we reduce the lock level on pg_subscription duringDROP SUBSCRIPTION from AccessExclusiveLock to RowExclusiveLock. Earlier,the higher lock level was used to prevent the launcher from starting a newworker during the drop operation, as a restarted worker could becomeorphaned.
Now, instead of relying on a strict lock, we acquire an AccessShareLock onthe specific subscription being dropped and re-validate its existenceafter acquiring the lock. If the subscription is no longer valid, theworker exits gracefully. This approach avoids the deadlock while stillensuring that orphan workers are not created.
Reported-by: Alexander Lakhin <exclusion(at)gmail(dot)com>Author: Dilip Kumar <dilipbalaut(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: vignesh C <vignesh21(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Hayato Kuroda <kuroda(dot)hayato(at)fujitsu(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Amit Kapila <amit(dot)kapila16(at)gmail(dot)com>Backpatch-through: 13Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18988-7312c868be2d467f@postgresql.org

before_code: * Lock pg_subscription with AccessExclusiveLock to ensure that the
* launcher doesn't restart new worker during dropping the subscription
after_code: * The launcher may concurrently start a new worker for this subscription.
* During initialization, the worker checks for subscription validity and
* exits if the subscription has already been dropped. See
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/commands/subscriptioncmds.c b/src/backend/commands/subscriptioncmds.c

index 4ff246cd94321ee426c00c6d5ef98673e39b201d..540f23ee590b64e217c7870c36f03c10e5239b19 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/commands/subscriptioncmds.c
+++ b/src/backend/commands/subscriptioncmds.c
@@ -1645,10 +1645,12 @@ DropSubscription(DropSubscriptionStmt *stmt, bool isTopLevel)
    bool        must_use_password;
 
    /*
-    * Lock pg_subscription with AccessExclusiveLock to ensure that the
-    * launcher doesn't restart new worker during dropping the subscription
+    * The launcher may concurrently start a new worker for this subscription.
+    * During initialization, the worker checks for subscription validity and
+    * exits if the subscription has already been dropped. See
+    * InitializeLogRepWorker.
     */
-   rel = table_open(SubscriptionRelationId, AccessExclusiveLock);
+   rel = table_open(SubscriptionRelationId, RowExclusiveLock);
 
    tup = SearchSysCache2(SUBSCRIPTIONNAME, MyDatabaseId,
                          CStringGetDatum(stmt->subname));

removed: * Lock pg_subscription with AccessExclusiveLock to ensure that the
* launcher doesn't restart new worker during dropping the subscription
added: * The launcher may concurrently start a new worker for this subscription.
* During initialization, the worker checks for subscription validity and
* exits if the subscription has already been dropped. See

subject: pgsql: Fix inconsistent quoting of role names in ACLs.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix inconsistent quoting of role names in ACLs.
getid() and putid(), which parse and deparse role names within ACLinput/output, applied isalnum() to see if a character within a rolename requires quoting.  They did this even for non-ASCII characters,which is problematic because the results would depend on encoding,locale, and perhaps even platform.  So it's possible that putid()could elect not to quote some string that, later in some otherenvironment, getid() will decide is not a valid identifier, causingdump/reload or similar failures.
To fix this in a way that won't risk interoperability problemswith unpatched versions, make getid() treat any non-ASCII as alegitimate identifier character (hence not requiring quotes),while making putid() treat any non-ASCII as requiring quoting.We could remove the resulting excess quoting once we feel thatno unpatched servers remain in the wild, but that'll be years.
A lesser problem is that getid() did the wrong thing with an inputconsisting of just two double quotes ("").  That has to represent anempty string, but getid() read it as a single double quote instead.The case cannot arise in the normal course of events, since we don'tallow empty-string role names.  But let's fix it while we're here.
Although we've not heard field reports of problems with non-ASCIIrole names, there's clearly a hazard there, so back-patch to allsupported versions.
Reported-by: Peter Eisentraut <peter(at)eisentraut(dot)org>Author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/3792884.1751492172@sss.pgh.pa.usBackpatch-through: 13

/* This test had better match what getid() does, above */

* Test whether an identifier char can be left unquoted in ACLs.
* Formerly, we used isalnum() even on non-ASCII characters, resulting in
* unportable behavior.  To ensure dump compatibility with old versions,
* we now treat high-bit-set characters as always requiring quoting during
* putid(), but getid() will always accept them without quotes.
*     Consumes the first alphanumeric string (identifier) found in string
/* safe to look at next char (could be '\0' though) */
/* it's an escaped double quote; skip the escaping char */
/* Detect whether we need to use double quotes */

subject: pgsql: Keep WAL segments by the flushed value of the slot's restart LSN
author: Alexander Korotkov <akorotkov(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Keep WAL segments by the flushed value of the slot's restart LSN
The patch fixes the issue with the unexpected removal of old WAL segmentsafter checkpoint, followed by an immediate restart.  The issue occurs whena slot is advanced after the start of the checkpoint and before old WALsegments are removed at the end of the checkpoint.
The idea of the patch is to get the minimal restart_lsn at the beginningof checkpoint (or restart point) creation and use this value when calculatingthe oldest LSN for WAL segments removal at the end of checkpoint.  This ideawas proposed by Tomas Vondra in the discussion.  Unlike 291221c46575, thisfix doesn't affect ABI and is intended for back branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/flat/1d12d2-67235980-35-19a406a0%4063439497Author: Vitaly Davydov <v(dot)davydov(at)postgrespro(dot)ru>Reviewed-by: Tomas Vondra <tomas(at)vondra(dot)me>Reviewed-by: Alexander Korotkov <aekorotkov(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Amit Kapila <amit(dot)kapila16(at)gmail(dot)com>Backpatch-through: 13

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test that WAL segments are retained based on the minimum LSN captured at checkpoint start, not after slot synchronization, by creating a replication slot, advancing it during checkpoint, and verifying old WAL segments are not prematurely removed.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_1') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_1');
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('test_slot_1');
-- Generate some WAL
CREATE TABLE test_wal_retention (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_wal_retention (data) SELECT 'dummy' FROM generate_series(1, 1000);
-- Get current LSN
SELECT pg_current_wal_lsn() AS start_lsn \gset
-- Advance slot to current LSN
SELECT pg_replication_slot_advance('test_slot_1', :'start_lsn');
-- Force a checkpoint to capture slotsMinReqLSN early
CHECKPOINT;
-- Immediately advance slot further after checkpoint start but before WAL removal
INSERT INTO test_wal_retention (data) SELECT 'more' FROM generate_series(1, 1000);
SELECT pg_replication_slot_advance('test_slot_1', pg_current_wal_lsn());
-- Force another checkpoint to trigger WAL removal with previously captured slotsMinReqLSN
CHECKPOINT;
-- Verify slot still exists and WAL hasn't been incorrectly removed
SELECT slot_name, restart_lsn FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_1';

-- Teardown
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_1');
DROP TABLE test_wal_retention;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test the recalculation of slotsMinReqLSN after slot invalidation during checkpoint, ensuring that after invalidating a slot due to max_slot_wal_keep_size, a new minimum LSN is computed and used for subsequent WAL removal.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_inval') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_inval');
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('test_slot_inval');
-- Generate substantial WAL to potentially exceed max_slot_wal_keep_size
CREATE TABLE test_inval (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, payload TEXT);
DO $$
BEGIN
    FOR i IN 1..10000 LOOP
        INSERT INTO test_inval (payload) VALUES (md5(random()::text));
    END LOOP;
END $$;
-- Advance slot to a recent LSN
SELECT pg_replication_slot_advance('test_slot_inval', pg_current_wal_lsn());
-- Set max_slot_wal_keep_size very low to force slot invalidation during checkpoint
SET max_slot_wal_keep_size = '1MB';
-- Force checkpoint which may invalidate slot and recalculate slotsMinReqLSN
CHECKPOINT;
-- Verify slot may be invalidated or still present depending on WAL generation
SELECT slot_name, active FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_inval';

-- Teardown
RESET max_slot_wal_keep_size;
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_inval') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_inval');
DROP TABLE test_inval;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test that GetWALAvailability uses the new slotsMinReqLSN parameter in KeepLogSeg to correctly report WAL availability considering replication slots, by creating a slot and checking WAL availability before and after slot advancement.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_avail') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_avail');
SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('test_slot_avail');
-- Generate initial WAL
CREATE TABLE test_avail (id INT);
INSERT INTO test_avail SELECT generate_series(1, 500);
-- Get current LSN and check WAL availability
SELECT pg_current_wal_lsn() AS curr_lsn \gset
SELECT pg_walfile_name(:'curr_lsn') AS curr_wal_file;
-- Advance slot to current LSN
SELECT pg_replication_slot_advance('test_slot_avail', :'curr_lsn');
-- Check WAL availability for a target LSN slightly before current
SELECT pg_wal_lsn_diff(:'curr_lsn', '0/0') AS bytes_generated;
SELECT pg_wal_lsn_diff(:'curr_lsn', restart_lsn) AS slot_lag FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_avail';
-- Use pg_get_wal_availability to verify it accounts for slot's restart LSN
SELECT * FROM pg_get_wal_availability(:'curr_lsn');

-- Teardown
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_avail');
DROP TABLE test_avail;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test restart point behavior with the new slotsMinReqLSN capture, simulating a standby where restart points are created, ensuring WAL segments are kept based on the minimum LSN captured at restart point start.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: This test requires running on a standby or using a hack; we'll simulate by checking if in recovery.
-- Since we cannot force a restart point on primary, we'll check the function logic indirectly.
-- Create a replication slot on primary if not in recovery.
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery() AS in_recovery \gset
\if :in_recovery
    -- If in recovery, we can test restart point indirectly by monitoring WAL.
    -- Create a temporary table to generate WAL on primary (not possible on standby).
    -- Instead, we'll just verify that slots are considered in recovery.
    SELECT slot_name, restart_lsn FROM pg_replication_slots LIMIT 1;
\else
    -- On primary, create a slot and generate WAL, then force a restart point isn't applicable.
    -- Instead, we'll test checkpoint which also uses the new logic.
    SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_restart') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_restart');
    SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('test_slot_restart');
    CREATE TABLE test_restart (id SERIAL);
    INSERT INTO test_restart SELECT generate_series(1, 1000);
    -- Advance slot
    SELECT pg_replication_slot_advance('test_slot_restart', pg_current_wal_lsn());
    -- Force checkpoint (simulating restart point logic for primary)
    CHECKPOINT;
    -- Verify slot persists
    SELECT slot_name, active FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_restart';
    -- Teardown
    SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_restart');
    DROP TABLE test_restart;
\endif
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test edge case where no replication slots exist, ensuring slotsMinReqLSN is InvalidXLogRecPtr and KeepLogSeg handles it correctly without affecting WAL removal.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Ensure no test slots exist
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('test_slot_none') WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_replication_slots WHERE slot_name = 'test_slot_none');
-- Generate WAL without any slots
CREATE TABLE test_no_slots (id INT);
INSERT INTO test_no_slots SELECT generate_series(1, 2000);
-- Force a checkpoint; with no slots, slotsMinReqLSN should be invalid
CHECKPOINT;
-- Verify that WAL removal proceeds normally by checking pg_ls_waldir for oldest segments (optional, may require superuser)
-- Instead, we can verify that the system operates without error.
SELECT 'Checkpoint completed without slots' AS verification;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE test_no_slots;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: static void KeepLogSeg(XLogRecPtr recptr, XLogRecPtr slotsMinLSN,

subject: pgsql: Fix uninitialized index information access during apply.
author: Amit Kapila <akapila(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix uninitialized index information access during apply.
The issue happens when building conflict information during apply ofINSERT or UPDATE operations that violate unique constraints on leafpartitions.
The problem was introduced in commit 9ff68679b5, which removed theredundant calls to ExecOpenIndices/ExecCloseIndices. The previous code wasrelying on the redundant ExecOpenIndices call inapply_handle_tuple_routing() to build the index information required forunique key conflict detection.
The fix is to delay building the index information until a conflict isdetected instead of relying on ExecOpenIndices to do the same. Theadditional benefit of this approach is that it avoids building indexinformation when there is no conflict.
Author: Hou Zhijie <houzj(dot)fnst(at)fujitsu(dot)com>Reviewed-by:Reviewed-by: Amit Kapila <amit(dot)kapila16(at)gmail(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/TYAPR01MB57244ADA33DDA57119B9D26494A62@TYAPR01MB5724.jpnprd01.prod.outlook.com

before_code: * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion or conflict
* detection in logical replication, add extra information required by
after_code: * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion, add extra
* information required by unique index entries.
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/executor/execIndexing.c b/src/backend/executor/execIndexing.c

index e3fe9b78bb5da7ff22087a238c70f73b747df130..bdf862b24062e74e0fd99c9adaa3416bebc6ea8c 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/executor/execIndexing.c
+++ b/src/backend/executor/execIndexing.c
@@ -214,9 +214,8 @@ ExecOpenIndices(ResultRelInfo *resultRelInfo, bool speculative)
        ii = BuildIndexInfo(indexDesc);
 
        /*
-        * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion or conflict
-        * detection in logical replication, add extra information required by
-        * unique index entries.
+        * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion, add extra
+        * information required by unique index entries.
         */
        if (speculative && ii->ii_Unique && !indexDesc->rd_index->indisexclusion)
            BuildSpeculativeIndexInfo(indexDesc, ii);

removed: * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion or conflict
* detection in logical replication, add extra information required by
added: * If the indexes are to be used for speculative insertion, add extra
* information required by unique index entries.

subject: pgsql: Remove XLogFileInit() ability to skip ControlFileLock.
author: Michael Paquier <michael(at)paquier(dot)xyz>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Remove XLogFileInit() ability to skip ControlFileLock.
Cold paths, initdb and end-of-recovery, used it.  Don't optimize them.
This commit has been applied as of c53c6b98d38a in v15 and newerversions.  This is required on stable branches of v13 and v14 to fix aregression reported by Noah Misch, introduced by 1f95181b44c8, causingspurious failures in archive recovery (neither streaming nor archiverecovery) with concurrent restartpoints.  The backpatched versions ofthe patches have been aligned on these branches by me, Noah Misch is theauthor.  Tests have been conducted by the both of us.

<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test concurrent restartpoints during archive recovery by simulating WAL segment creation under load.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a test table and generate some WAL activity
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_wal CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_concurrent_wal (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_concurrent_wal (data) SELECT 'payload' || g FROM generate_series(1, 1000) g;
CHECKPOINT;

-- Execution: Force WAL segment switch and concurrent checkpoint
SELECT pg_switch_wal();
-- Simulate concurrent restartpoint by forcing a checkpoint in a separate session via dblink
DO $$
BEGIN
    PERFORM dblink_connect('host=localhost dbname=' || current_database());
    PERFORM dblink_exec('CHECKPOINT');
    PERFORM dblink_disconnect();
EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN
    -- dblink might not be available, ignore
END $$;

-- Verify no errors in logs by checking if table is accessible
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_concurrent_wal;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_wal CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test XLogFileInit during bootstrap-like scenario by creating a new database which triggers WAL segment initialization.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Ensure clean state
DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS test_bootstrap_db;
-- Execution: Create a new database which will invoke WAL operations
CREATE DATABASE test_bootstrap_db;
-- Connect to new database and create a table to generate WAL
\c test_bootstrap_db
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_bootstrap_tbl CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_bootstrap_tbl (id INT);
INSERT INTO test_bootstrap_tbl VALUES (1);
CHECKPOINT;
-- Switch back to original database
\c postgres

-- Teardown
DROP DATABASE test_bootstrap_db;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test InstallXLogFileSegment with find_free=false by forcing WAL recycling via checkpoint segments.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Adjust checkpoint segments to force recycling
ALTER SYSTEM SET checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9;
ALTER SYSTEM SET checkpoint_segments = 3; -- Only works if using older parameter style, use checkpoint_timeout instead
SELECT pg_reload_conf();
-- Create WAL activity
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_recycle CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_wal_recycle (id SERIAL, val TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_wal_recycle (val) SELECT 'data' || i FROM generate_series(1, 5000) i;

-- Execution: Force multiple WAL switches and checkpoints to trigger recycling
DO $$
BEGIN
    FOR i IN 1..10 LOOP
        PERFORM pg_switch_wal();
        PERFORM pg_sleep(0.1);
    END LOOP;
END $$;
CHECKPOINT;

-- Verify system is operational
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_wal_recycle;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_recycle CASCADE;
ALTER SYSTEM RESET checkpoint_completion_target;
ALTER SYSTEM RESET checkpoint_segments;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test end-of-recovery path by simulating recovery and forcing a new timeline creation.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a base backup scenario
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_tbl CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_recovery_tbl (id INT PRIMARY KEY);
INSERT INTO test_recovery_tbl VALUES (1);
-- Take a base backup (simulate by checkpoint and WAL switch)
CHECKPOINT;
SELECT pg_switch_wal();

-- Execution: Simulate recovery completion by promoting a standby (if in recovery mode)
-- This is a simulation; actual promotion requires a standby setup.
-- Instead, we force a timeline switch by creating a restore point and recovering.
DO $$
BEGIN
    IF pg_is_in_recovery() THEN
        PERFORM pg_create_restore_point('test_recovery_point');
        -- Promote if possible (this will fail if not a standby)
        PERFORM pg_promote();
    END IF;
EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN
    -- Not in recovery, ignore
END $$;

-- Verify data integrity post-recovery simulation
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_recovery_tbl WHERE id = 1;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_tbl CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test ControlFileLock acquisition during concurrent WAL segment creation by simulating multiple sessions creating WAL activity.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a table for WAL generation
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_lock CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_concurrent_lock (id SERIAL, payload TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_concurrent_lock (payload) SELECT 'concurrent' || g FROM generate_series(1, 100) g;

-- Execution: Simulate concurrent sessions via background workers or dblink
-- Use dblink to run concurrent checkpoints and WAL switches
DO $$
DECLARE
    conn text;
BEGIN
    conn := 'host=localhost dbname=' || current_database();
    -- Session 1: Force WAL switch
    PERFORM dblink_exec(conn, 'SELECT pg_switch_wal()');
    -- Session 2: Concurrent checkpoint
    PERFORM dblink_exec(conn, 'CHECKPOINT');
EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN
    -- dblink not available, use simple sequential operations
    PERFORM pg_switch_wal();
    CHECKPOINT;
END $$;

-- Verify no lock conflicts by checking system health
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active';

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_lock CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Remove XLogFileInit() ability to unlink a pre-existing file.
author: Michael Paquier <michael(at)paquier(dot)xyz>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Remove XLogFileInit() ability to unlink a pre-existing file.
Only initdb used it.  initdb refuses to operate on a non-empty directoryand generally does not cope with pre-existing files of other kinds.Hence, use the opportunity to simplify.
This commit has been applied as of 421484f79c0b in v15 and newerversions.  This is required on stable branches of v13 and v14 to fix aregression reported by Noah Misch, introduced by 1f95181b44c8, causingspurious failures in archive recovery (neither streaming nor archiverecovery) with concurrent restartpoints.  The backpatched versions ofthe patches have been aligned on these branches by me, Noah Misch is theauthor.  Tests have been conducted by the both of us.
Reported-by: Arun ThirupathiAuthor: Noah Misch <noah(at)leadboat(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/20210202151416.GB3304930@rfd.leadboat.comDiscussion: https://postgr.es/m/20250306193013.36.nmisch@google.comBackpatch-through: 13

    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test archive recovery scenario where XLogFileInit is called with a pre-existing segment file to ensure no spurious failures.</description>

-- Note: This test requires superuser privileges and careful handling
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_setup CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_recovery_setup (note TEXT);
-- Create a dummy WAL segment file in pg_wal (requires superuser)
DO $$
BEGIN
    EXECUTE 'CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION create_dummy_wal_segment() RETURNS void AS $$
        DECLARE
            segname text;
            segpath text;
        BEGIN
            segname := ''000000010000000000000001'';
            segpath := pg_catalog.current_setting(''data_directory'') || ''/pg_wal/'' || segname;
            PERFORM pg_catalog.pg_file_unlink(segpath);
            PERFORM pg_catalog.pg_file_write(segpath, ''\\x00000000'', false);
        END;
    $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER';
    PERFORM create_dummy_wal_segment();
    DROP FUNCTION create_dummy_wal_segment();
EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN
    INSERT INTO test_recovery_setup VALUES ('Skipping due to permissions: ' || SQLERRM);
END $$;

-- Execution: Attempt to open the existing segment (simulating recovery)
-- This is indirect; we rely on the system not crashing
SELECT 1 AS recovery_test_passed WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_ls_waldir() WHERE name = '000000010000000000000001');

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_recovery_setup CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>

        <sql>

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_segment_added CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_wal_segment_added (id SERIAL, payload TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_wal_segment_added (payload) SELECT repeat('x', 1000) FROM generate_series(1, 5000);

-- Execution: Force a checkpoint and monitor pg_stat_checkpoint for segments added
CHECKPOINT;
SELECT ckpt_segs_added > 0 AS segments_were_added FROM pg_stat_checkpoint;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_wal_segment_added CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test bootstrap initialization (initdb scenario) where XLogFileInit is called without pre-existing files, ensuring it works after the removal of the unlink ability.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: This test simulates a bootstrap-like environment by creating a new tablespace and generating WAL there
DROP TABLESPACE IF EXISTS test_bootstrap_tbsp CASCADE;
CREATE TABLESPACE test_bootstrap_tbsp LOCATION '/tmp/pg_test_bootstrap';
SET default_tablespace = 'test_bootstrap_tbsp';

INSERT INTO test_bootstrap_table VALUES (1), (2), (3);

        </sql>

        <sql>

CREATE TABLE test_concurrent_restartpoint AS SELECT generate_series(1, 10000) AS id, md5(random()::text) AS data;

-- Execution: Perform multiple operations while forcing restartpoints via configuration
-- Temporarily reduce checkpoint_timeout to increase checkpoint frequency

SELECT pg_reload_conf();

DELETE FROM test_concurrent_restartpoint WHERE id % 3 = 0;
-- Restore default
ALTER SYSTEM RESET checkpoint_timeout;
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

-- Verification: Ensure no errors occurred and WAL directory is accessible
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_wal_files FROM pg_ls_waldir();

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_concurrent_restartpoint CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

* filling ours: if so, use ours to pre-create a future log segment.

subject: pgsql: Don't ERROR on PreallocXlogFiles() race condition.
author: Michael Paquier <michael(at)paquier(dot)xyz>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Don't ERROR on PreallocXlogFiles() race condition.
Before a restartpoint finishes PreallocXlogFiles(), a startup processKeepFileRestoredFromArchive() call can unlink the preallocated segment.If a CHECKPOINT sql command had elicited the restartpoint experiencingthe race condition, that sql command failed.  Moreover, the restartpointomitted its log_checkpoints message and some inessential resourcereclamation.  Prevent the ERROR by skipping open() of the segment.Since these consequences are so minor, no back-patch.
This commit has been applied as of 2b3e4672f760 in v15 and newerversions.  This is required on stable branches of v13 and v14 to fix aregression reported by Noah Misch, introduced by 1f95181b44c8, causingspurious failures in archive recovery (neither streaming nor archiverecovery) with concurrent restartpoints.  The backpatched versions ofthe patches have been aligned on these branches by me, Noah Misch is theauthor.  Tests have been conducted by the both of us.

subject: pgsql: Skip WAL recycling and preallocation during archive recovery.
author: Michael Paquier <michael(at)paquier(dot)xyz>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Skip WAL recycling and preallocation during archive recovery.
The previous commit addressed the chief consequences of a race conditionbetween InstallXLogFileSegment() and KeepFileRestoredFromArchive().  Fixthree lesser consequences.  A spurious durable_rename_excl() LOG messageremained possible.  KeepFileRestoredFromArchive() wasted the proceeds ofWAL recycling and preallocation.  Finally, XLogFileInitInternal() couldreturn a descriptor for a file that KeepFileRestoredFromArchive() hadalready unlinked.  That felt like a recipe for future bugs.
This commit has been applied as of cc2c7d65fc27 in v15 and newerversions.  This is required on stable branches of v13 and v14 to fix aregression reported by Noah Misch, introduced by 1f95181b44c8, causingspurious failures in archive recovery (neither streaming nor archiverecovery) with concurrent restartpoints.  The backpatched versions ofthe patches have been aligned on these branches by me, Noah Misch is theauthor.  Tests have been conducted by the both of us.
Note that this commit is known to have introduced a regression of itsown.  This is fixed by the commit following this one, and not grouped ina single commit to keep the commit history consistent across allbranches.

subject: pgsql: Fix parse_cte.c's failure to examine sub-WITHs in DML statements
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix parse_cte.c's failure to examine sub-WITHs in DML statements.
makeDependencyGraphWalker thought that only SelectStmt nodes couldcontain a WithClause.  Which was true in our original implementationof WITH, but astonishingly we missed updating this code when we addedthe ability to attach WITH to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (and later MERGE).Moreover, since it was coded to deliberately block recursion to aWithClause, even updating raw_expression_tree_walker didn't save it.
The upshot of this was that we didn't see references to outer CTEnames appearing within an inner WITH, and would neither complain aboutdisallowed recursion nor account for such references when sorting CTEsinto a usable order.  The lack of complaints about this is perhaps notso surprising, because typical usage of WITH wouldn't hit either case.Still, it's pretty broken; failing to detect recursion here leads toassert failures or worse later on.
Fix by factoring out the processing of sub-WITHs into a new functionWalkInnerWith, and invoking that for all the statement types thatcan have WITH.
Bug: #18878Reported-by: Yu Liang <luy70(at)psu(dot)edu>Author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18878-a26fa5ab6be2f2cf@postgresql.orgBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: static void TopologicalSort(ParseState *pstate, CteItem *items, int numitems);
* In the RECURSIVE case, all query names of the WITH are
* visible to all WITH items as well as the main query. So
* push them all on, process, pop them all off.
* In the non-RECURSIVE case, query names are visible to the
* WITH items after them and to the main query.
/* note that recursion could mutate innerwiths list */
/* if no WITH clause, just fall through for normal processing */
* Prevent raw_expression_tree_walker from recursing directly into a
* Sort by dependencies, using a standard topological sort operation

* all WITH items as well as the main query.  So push them all on,
* In the non-RECURSIVE case, query names are visible to the WITH
* items after them and to the main query.

INSERT INTO test_update VALUES (1, 0), (2, 0);

-- Execution: Recursive CTE in UPDATE with inner WITH
WITH RECURSIVE rcte AS (
    SELECT 1 AS level
    UNION ALL
    SELECT level + 1 FROM rcte WHERE level < 3
)
UPDATE test_update t
SET val = r.level
FROM (
    WITH inner_cte AS (SELECT level FROM rcte)
    SELECT * FROM inner_cte
) r
WHERE t.id = r.level;

INSERT INTO source_table VALUES (1, 'new'), (3, 'new');

-- Execution: MERGE with CTE containing inner WITH
WITH cte_source AS (SELECT * FROM source_table)
MERGE INTO target_table t
USING (
    WITH inner_cte AS (SELECT * FROM cte_source)
    SELECT * FROM inner_cte
) s
ON t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET val = s.val
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT VALUES (s.id, s.val);

-- Verify
SELECT * FROM target_table ORDER BY id;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS target_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS source_table CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test complex nested WITH clauses in SELECT with recursion detection across DML statements</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_complex CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_complex (a INT, b INT);
INSERT INTO test_complex VALUES (1, 100), (2, 200);

-- Execution: Multiple levels of WITH in UPDATE referencing outer CTEs
WITH outer1 AS (SELECT 1 AS key),
     outer2 AS (SELECT key + 1 AS key FROM outer1)
UPDATE test_complex t
SET b = (
    WITH inner1 AS (SELECT key FROM outer1),
         inner2 AS (SELECT key FROM outer2)
    SELECT i1.key * 1000 + i2.key * 100
    FROM inner1 i1, inner2 i2
)
WHERE t.a = 1;

-- Verify
SELECT * FROM test_complex ORDER BY a;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_complex CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Relax assertion in finding correct GiST parent
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Relax assertion in finding correct GiST parent
Commit 28d3c2ddcf introduced an assertion that if the memorizeddownlink location in the insertion stack isn't valid, the parent'sLSN should've changed too. Turns out that was too strict. IngistFindCorrectParent(), if we walk right, we update the parent'sblock number and clear its memorized 'downlinkoffnum'. That triggeredthe assertion on next call to gistFindCorrectParent(), if the parentneeded to be split too. Relax the assertion, so that it's OK ifdownlinkOffnum is InvalidOffsetNumber.
Backpatch to v13-, all supported versions. The assertion was added incommit 28d3c2ddcf in v12.

subject: pgsql: Remove unnecessary type violation in tsvectorrecv().
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Remove unnecessary type violation in tsvectorrecv().
compareentry() is declared to work on WordEntryIN structs, buttsvectorrecv() is using it in two places to work on WordEntrystructs.  This is almost okay, since WordEntry is the firstfield of WordEntryIN.  But on machines with 8-byte pointers,WordEntryIN will have a larger alignment spec than WordEntry,and it's at least theoretically possible that the compilercould generate code that depends on the larger alignment.
Given the lack of field reports, this may be just a hypothetical bugthat upsets nothing except sanitizer tools.  Or it may be real oncertain hardware but nobody's tried to use tsvectorrecv() on suchhardware.  In any case we should fix it, and the fix is trivial:just change compareentry() so that it works on WordEntry without anymention of WordEntryIN.  We can also get rid of the quite-uselessintermediate function WordEntryCMP.
Bug: #18875Reported-by: Alexander Lakhin <exclusion(at)gmail(dot)com>Author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18875-07a29c49c825a608@postgresql.orgBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: int         poslen;         /* number of elements in pos */
/* Compare two WordEntryIN values for qsort */
after_code: WordEntry   entry;          /* must be first, see compareentry */
int         poslen;         /* number of elements in pos */
* Compare two WordEntry structs for qsort_arg.  This can also be used on
* WordEntryIN structs, since those have WordEntry as their first field.

added: WordEntry   entry;          /* must be first, see compareentry */

removed: /* Compare two WordEntryIN values for qsort */
added: * Compare two WordEntry structs for qsort_arg.  This can also be used on
* WordEntryIN structs, since those have WordEntry as their first field.
