subject: pgsql: Build whole-row Vars the same way during parsing and planning.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Build whole-row Vars the same way during parsing and planning.
makeWholeRowVar() has different rules for constructing awhole-row Var depending on the kind of RTE it's representing.This turns out to be problematic because the rewriter and plannercan convert view RTEs and set-returning-function RTEs intosubquery RTEs; so a whole-row Var made during planning mightlook different from one made by the parser.  In isolation thisdoesn't cause any problem, but if a query contains Vars madeboth ways for the same varno, there are cross-checks in theexecutor that will complain.  This manifests for UPDATE, DELETE,and MERGE queries that use whole-row table references.
To fix, we need makeWholeRowVar() to produce the same resultfrom an inlined RTE as it would have for the original.  Foran inlined view, we can use RangeTblEntry.relid to detectthat this had been a view RTE.  For inlined SRFs, make adata structure definition change akin to commit 47bb9db75,and say that we won't clear RangeTblEntry.functions untilthe end of planning.  That allows makeWholeRowVar() torepeat what it would have done with the unmodified RTE.
Reported-by: Duncan Sands <duncan(dot)sands(at)deepbluecap(dot)com>Reported-by: Dean Rasheed <dean(dot)a(dot)rasheed(at)gmail(dot)com>Diagnosed-by: Tender Wang <tndrwang(at)gmail(dot)com>Author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Reviewed-by: Dean Rasheed <dean(dot)a(dot)rasheed(at)gmail(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/3518c50a-ab18-482f-b916-a37263622501@deepbluecap.comBackpatch-through: 13

before_code: * RTE is a join, subselect, tablefunc, or VALUES.  We represent
* this as a whole-row Var of RECORD type. (Note that in most
* cases the Var will be expanded to a RowExpr during planning,
* but that is not our concern here.)
after_code: * For a standard subquery, the Var should be of RECORD type.
* However, if we're looking at a subquery that was expanded from
* a view or SRF (only possible during planning), we must use the
* appropriate rowtype, so that the resulting Var has the same
* type that we would have produced from the original RTE.
/* Subquery was expanded from a view */
errmsg("relation \"%s\" does not have a composite type",
* Subquery was expanded from a set-returning function.  That
* would not have happened if there's more than one function
* or ordinality was requested.  We also needn't worry about
* the allowScalar case, since the planner doesn't use that.
* Otherwise this must match the RTE_FUNCTION code below.
* RTE is a join, tablefunc, VALUES, CTE, etc.  We represent these
* cases as a whole-row Var of RECORD type.  (Note that in most
* cases the Var will be expanded to a RowExpr during planning,
* but that is not our concern here.)
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/nodes/makefuncs.c b/src/backend/nodes/makefuncs.c

index 0e7e6e46d943b79c36825ee0f47fbad6eccd46d1..6ed16254c93e3d9fd5a75c0eb1c7dea923c4b257 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/nodes/makefuncs.c
+++ b/src/backend/nodes/makefuncs.c
@@ -160,6 +160,53 @@ makeWholeRowVar(RangeTblEntry *rte,
                             varlevelsup);
            break;
 
+       case RTE_SUBQUERY:
+
+           /*
+            * For a standard subquery, the Var should be of RECORD type.
+            * However, if we're looking at a subquery that was expanded from
+            * a view or SRF (only possible during planning), we must use the
+            * appropriate rowtype, so that the resulting Var has the same
+            * type that we would have produced from the original RTE.
+            */
+           if (OidIsValid(rte->relid))
+           {
+               /* Subquery was expanded from a view */
+               toid = get_rel_type_id(rte->relid);
+               if (!OidIsValid(toid))
+                   ereport(ERROR,
+                           (errcode(ERRCODE_WRONG_OBJECT_TYPE),
+                            errmsg("relation \"%s\" does not have a composite type",
+                                   get_rel_name(rte->relid))));
+           }
+           else if (rte->functions)
+           {
+               /*
+                * Subquery was expanded from a set-returning function.  That
+                * would not have happened if there's more than one function
+                * or ordinality was requested.  We also needn't worry about
+                * the allowScalar case, since the planner doesn't use that.
+                * Otherwise this must match the RTE_FUNCTION code below.
+                */
+               Assert(!allowScalar);
+               fexpr = ((RangeTblFunction *) linitial(rte->functions))->funcexpr;
+               toid = exprType(fexpr);
+               if (!type_is_rowtype(toid))
+                   toid = RECORDOID;
+           }
+           else
+           {
+               /* Normal subquery-in-FROM */
+               toid = RECORDOID;
+           }
+           result = makeVar(varno,
+                            InvalidAttrNumber,
+                            toid,
+                            -1,
+                            InvalidOid,
+                            varlevelsup);
+           break;
+
        case RTE_FUNCTION:
 
            /*
@@ -216,8 +263,8 @@ makeWholeRowVar(RangeTblEntry *rte,
        default:
 
            /*
-            * RTE is a join, subselect, tablefunc, or VALUES.  We represent
-            * this as a whole-row Var of RECORD type. (Note that in most
+            * RTE is a join, tablefunc, VALUES, CTE, etc.  We represent these
+            * cases as a whole-row Var of RECORD type.  (Note that in most
             * cases the Var will be expanded to a RowExpr during planning,
             * but that is not our concern here.)
             */

* However, if we're looking at a subquery that was expanded from
* a view or SRF (only possible during planning), we must use the
* appropriate rowtype, so that the resulting Var has the same
* type that we would have produced from the original RTE.

* would not have happened if there's more than one function

-- Setup

CREATE TABLE test_data (c1 INT, c2 CHAR(1));
INSERT INTO test_data VALUES (10, 'A'), (20, 'B');

-- Execution: Use whole-row reference to a plain subquery
SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM test_data) AS sub WHERE sub = ROW(10, 'A');

-- Teardown

        </sql>

        <sql>

DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_func1() CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_func2() CASCADE;
CREATE FUNCTION test_func1() RETURNS TABLE(a INT) AS $$ SELECT 1 $$ LANGUAGE SQL;
CREATE FUNCTION test_func2() RETURNS TABLE(b TEXT) AS $$ SELECT 'x' $$ LANGUAGE SQL;

-- Execution: Use whole-row reference on a FROM clause with multiple SRFs
SELECT * FROM test_func1(), test_func2() WHERE test_func1 = ROW(1);

-- Teardown
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_func1() CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_func2() CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test concurrent WAL buffer initialization by multiple backends inserting records simultaneously, verifying the reverted WALBufMappingLock handles contention correctly.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS concurrent_wal_test CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE concurrent_wal_test (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
SELECT pg_backend_pid() AS main_pid \gset

-- Execution: Create multiple sessions via dblink to simulate concurrent inserts
DO $$
DECLARE
    i INTEGER;
    result TEXT;
BEGIN
    -- Insert from current session
    FOR i IN 1..100 LOOP
        INSERT INTO concurrent_wal_test (data) VALUES (md5(random()::text));
    END LOOP;

    -- Use dblink for concurrent inserts from another backend
    IF (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_extension WHERE extname = 'dblink') = 0 THEN
        CREATE EXTENSION dblink;
    END IF;

    -- Perform concurrent inserts in a separate connection
    PERFORM dblink_connect('concurrent_conn', 'dbname=' || current_database());
    PERFORM dblink('concurrent_conn', 
        'INSERT INTO concurrent_wal_test (data) SELECT md5(random()::text) FROM generate_series(1,100)'
    );
    PERFORM dblink_disconnect('concurrent_conn');

    -- Verify data consistency
    IF (SELECT count(*) FROM concurrent_wal_test) = 200 THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Concurrent WAL insert test passed: 200 rows inserted';
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Concurrent WAL insert test failed: expected 200 rows';
    END IF;
END $$;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS concurrent_wal_test CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test WAL buffer page replacement under heavy write load with checkpoints, ensuring WALBufMappingLock properly serializes buffer initialization during flush.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS heavy_wal_test CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE heavy_wal_test (id BIGINT, payload TEXT);
ALTER TABLE heavy_wal_test SET (autovacuum_enabled = false);

-- Execution: Generate heavy WAL activity with forced checkpoints
DO $$
DECLARE
    checkpoint_segments INTEGER;
BEGIN
    -- Temporarily reduce checkpoint segments to trigger more frequent WAL flushes
    checkpoint_segments := current_setting('checkpoint_segments')::integer;
    PERFORM set_config('checkpoint_segments', '1', false);
    
    -- Insert large volume of data to fill WAL buffers
    FOR i IN 1..1000 LOOP
        INSERT INTO heavy_wal_test 
        SELECT g, repeat(md5(random()::text), 10) 
        FROM generate_series(1, 100) g;
        
        -- Force checkpoint every 100 iterations
        IF i % 100 = 0 THEN
            CHECKPOINT;
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
    
    -- Restore original setting
    PERFORM set_config('checkpoint_segments', checkpoint_segments::text, false);
    
    -- Verify data integrity
    IF (SELECT count(*) FROM heavy_wal_test) = 100000 THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Heavy WAL test passed: 100000 rows inserted with checkpoints';
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Heavy WAL test failed: row count mismatch';
    END IF;
END $$;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS heavy_wal_test CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test WAL buffer initialization edge case when crossing WAL buffer ring boundary, verifying pointer calculations work correctly with reverted lock.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS wal_boundary_test CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE wal_boundary_test (id INT, data TEXT);

-- Execution: Fill WAL buffers to force ring buffer wrap-around
DO $$
DECLARE
    wal_buffers INTEGER;
    total_records INTEGER;
BEGIN
    -- Get current WAL buffer size
    wal_buffers := current_setting('wal_buffers')::integer;
    -- Estimate records needed to fill buffers multiple times
    total_records := wal_buffers * 10;
    
    -- Insert enough data to cause multiple WAL buffer wraps
    FOR i IN 1..total_records LOOP
        INSERT INTO wal_boundary_test VALUES (i, md5(i::text));
        
        -- Periodically force WAL flush
        IF i % 1000 = 0 THEN
            PERFORM pg_current_wal_lsn();
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
    
    -- Verify all data persisted correctly
    IF (SELECT count(DISTINCT id) FROM wal_boundary_test) = total_records THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'WAL boundary test passed: % records across buffer boundaries', total_records;
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'WAL boundary test failed: missing records';
    END IF;
END $$;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS wal_boundary_test CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test opportunistic WAL buffer pre-initialization by WAL writer while other backends are actively inserting, verifying lock coordination.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS opportunistic_wal_test CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE opportunistic_wal_test (id SERIAL, ts TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now(), data TEXT);

-- Execution: Simulate mixed workload with concurrent inserts and WAL writer activity
DO $$
DECLARE
    start_lsn pg_lsn;
    end_lsn pg_lsn;
BEGIN
    -- Record starting WAL position
    start_lsn := pg_current_wal_lsn();
    
    -- Create concurrent insert workload
    FOR i IN 1..500 LOOP
        INSERT INTO opportunistic_wal_test (data) 
        SELECT md5(random()::text) FROM generate_series(1, 10);
        
        -- Force WAL writer activity periodically
        IF i % 50 = 0 THEN
            PERFORM pg_switch_wal();
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
    
    -- Record ending WAL position
    end_lsn := pg_current_wal_lsn();
    
    -- Verify WAL progressed
    IF end_lsn > start_lsn THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Opportunistic WAL test passed: WAL advanced from % to %', start_lsn, end_lsn;
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Opportunistic WAL test failed: WAL did not advance';
    END IF;
    
    -- Verify data integrity
    IF (SELECT count(*) FROM opportunistic_wal_test) = 5000 THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'All 5000 rows persisted correctly';
    END IF;
END $$;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS opportunistic_wal_test CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test recovery scenario with WAL buffer re-initialization, ensuring InitializedUpTo pointer is correctly set without atomic operations.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS recovery_wal_test CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE recovery_wal_test (id INT PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);

-- Execution: Create WAL activity then simulate crash recovery scenario
DO $$
BEGIN
    -- Generate some WAL activity
    INSERT INTO recovery_wal_test 
    SELECT g, 'data_' || g FROM generate_series(1, 1000) g;
    
    -- Force WAL flush
    PERFORM pg_switch_wal();
    
    -- Create unlogged table to test WAL buffer initialization after restart-like scenario
    DROP TABLE IF EXISTS unlogged_test CASCADE;
    CREATE UNLOGGED TABLE unlogged_test (id INT);
    
    -- Insert into unlogged table (generates WAL for init)
    INSERT INTO unlogged_test VALUES (1);
    
    -- Force checkpoint to ensure WAL buffers are written
    CHECKPOINT;
    
    -- More WAL activity after checkpoint
    INSERT INTO recovery_wal_test 
    SELECT g + 1000, 'data_' || (g + 1000) FROM generate_series(1, 500) g;
    
    -- Verify data persistence
    IF (SELECT count(*) FROM recovery_wal_test) = 1500 THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Recovery WAL test passed: 1500 rows persisted through WAL activity';
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Recovery WAL test failed: expected 1500 rows';
    END IF;
    
    -- Cleanup unlogged table
    DROP TABLE IF EXISTS unlogged_test CASCADE;
END $$;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS recovery_wal_test CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * WALBufMappingLock: must be held to replace a page in the WAL buffer cache.
* It is only held while initializing and changing the mapping.  If the
* contents of the buffer being replaced haven't been written yet, the mapping
* lock is released while the write is done, and reacquired afterwards.
* To change the identity of a buffer (and InitializedUpTo), you need to
* hold WALBufMappingLock.  To change the identity of a buffer that's
* page is not initialized yet, you have to grab WALBufMappingLock to
* initialize it, but the WAL writer tries to do that ahead of insertions
* to avoid that from happening in the critical path.

subject: pgsql: Fix setrefs.c's failure to do expression processing on prune ste
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix setrefs.c's failure to do expression processing on prune steps.
We should run the expression subtrees of PartitionedRelPruneInfostructs through fix_scan_expr.  Failure to do so means thatAlternativeSubPlans within those expressions won't be cleaned upproperly, resulting in "unrecognized node type" errors since v14.
It seems fairly likely that at least some of the other steps doneby fix_scan_expr are important here as well, resulting in as-yet-undetected bugs.  Therefore, I've chosen to back-patch this toall supported branches including v13, even though the knownsymptom doesn't manifest in v13.
Per bug #18778 from Alexander Lakhin.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18778-24cd399df6c806af@postgresql.org

subject: pgsql: Make getObjectDescription robust against dangling amproc type li
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Make getObjectDescription robust against dangling amproc type links.
Yoran Heling reported a case where a data type could be droppedwhile references to its OID remain behind in pg_amproc.  Thiscauses getObjectDescription to fail, which blocks dropping theoperator family (since our DROP code likes to construct descriptionsof everything it's dropping).  The proper fix for this requiresadding more pg_depend entries.  But to allow DROP to go through withalready-corrupt catalogs, tweak getObjectDescription to print "???"for the type instead of failing when it processes such an entry.
I changed the logic for pg_amop similarly, for consistency,although it is not known that the problem can manifest in pg_amop.
Per report from Yoran Heling.  Back-patch to all supportedbranches (although the problem may be unreachable in v13).
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/Z1MVCOh1hprjK5Sf@gmai021

subject: pgsql: Fix NULLIF()'s handling of read-write expanded objects.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix NULLIF()'s handling of read-write expanded objects.
If passed a read-write expanded object pointer, the EEOP_NULLIFcode would hand that same pointer to the equality functionand then (unless equality was reported) also return the samepointer as its value.  This is no good, because a function thatreceives a read-write expanded object pointer is fully entitledto scribble on or even delete the object, thus corrupting theNULLIF output.  (This problem is likely unobservable with theequality functions provided in core Postgres, but it's easy todemonstrate with one coded in plpgsql.)
To fix, make sure the pointer passed to the equality functionis read-only.  We can still return the original read-writepointer as the NULLIF result, allowing optimization of lateroperations.
Per bug #18722 from Alexander Lakhin.  This has been wrongsince we invented expanded objects, so back-patch to allsupported branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18722-fd9e645448cc78b4@postgresql.org

before_code: * Change opcode of call instruction to EEOP_NULLIF.
after_code: * If first argument is of varlena type, we'll need to ensure
* that the value passed to the comparison function is a
* Change opcode of call instruction to EEOP_NULLIF.
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/executor/execExpr.c b/src/backend/executor/execExpr.c

index 60f9eb28cd40befea6697a22ab8f8d235f328dff..ca0d38b3095794c08ff9b7ff8c1a8e4061f62da7 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/executor/execExpr.c
+++ b/src/backend/executor/execExpr.c
@@ -1170,6 +1170,14 @@ ExecInitExprRec(Expr *node, ExprState *state,
                             op->args, op->opfuncid, op->inputcollid,
                             state);
 
+               /*
+                * If first argument is of varlena type, we'll need to ensure
+                * that the value passed to the comparison function is a
+                * read-only pointer.
+                */
+               scratch.d.func.make_ro =
+                   (get_typlen(exprType((Node *) linitial(op->args))) == -1);
+
                /*
                 * Change opcode of call instruction to EEOP_NULLIF.
                 *

* that the value passed to the comparison function is a

<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test NULLIF with a read-write expanded object (like a JSONB) that could be mutated by a custom equality function, verifying the fix prevents corruption.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS mutable_eq(jsonb, jsonb) CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsonb CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsonb (data jsonb);
INSERT INTO test_jsonb VALUES ('{"a": 1}'::jsonb);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION mutable_eq(a jsonb, b jsonb)
RETURNS boolean
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Simulate mutation: modify the first argument (which would be read-write without fix)
    a = '{"mutated": true}'::jsonb;
    RETURN a = b;
END;
$$;

-- Execution: Use NULLIF with custom equality; result should be original jsonb, not mutated.
SELECT NULLIF(data, '{"a": 2}'::jsonb) FROM test_jsonb;

-- Teardown
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS mutable_eq(jsonb, jsonb) CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsonb CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>

        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test NULLIF with a read-write expanded object in a plpgsql function that modifies the argument, verifying the result is not corrupted.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_nullif_plpgsql() CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_plpgsql CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_plpgsql (j jsonb);
INSERT INTO test_plpgsql VALUES ('{"key": "value"}');

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION test_nullif_plpgsql()
RETURNS jsonb
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
DECLARE
    x jsonb;
    y jsonb;
BEGIN
    x := '{"key": "value"}'::jsonb;
    y := '{"different": "data"}'::jsonb;
    -- This should return x unchanged, even if equality function might mutate.
    RETURN NULLIF(x, y);
END;
$$;

-- Execution
SELECT test_nullif_plpgsql();

-- Teardown
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_nullif_plpgsql() CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_plpgsql CASCADE;
        </sql>

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_comp CASCADE;
CREATE TYPE comp_type AS (id int, data jsonb);
CREATE TABLE test_comp (col comp_type);
INSERT INTO test_comp VALUES ((1, '{"a":1}'::jsonb));

DROP TYPE IF EXISTS comp_type CASCADE;
        </sql>

subject: pgsql: Transform OR-clauses to SAOP's during index matching
author: Alexander Korotkov <akorotkov(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Transform OR-clauses to SAOP's during index matching
This commit makes match_clause_to_indexcol() match"(indexkey op C1) OR (indexkey op C2) ... (indexkey op CN)" expressionto the index while transforming it into "indexkey op ANY(ARRAY[C1, C2, ...])"(ScalarArrayOpExpr node).
This transformation allows handling long OR-clauses with single IndexScanavoiding diving them into a slower BitmapOr.
We currently restrict Ci to be either Const or Param to apply thistransformation only when it's clearly beneficial.  However, in the future,we might switch to a liberal understanding of constants, as it is in othercases.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/567ED6CA.2040504%40sigaev.ruAuthor: Alena Rybakina, Andrey Lepikhov, Alexander KorotkovReviewed-by: Peter Geoghegan, Ranier Vilela, Alexander Korotkov, Robert HaasReviewed-by: Jian He, Tom Lane, Nikolay Shaplov

*   Our definition of "const" is exceedingly liberal: we allow anything that
*   doesn't involve a volatile function or a Var of the index's relation.
*   In particular, Vars belonging to other relations of the query are
*   accepted here, since a clause of that form can be used in a
*   parameterized indexscan.  It's the responsibility of higher code levels
*   It is also possible to match ScalarArrayOpExpr clauses to indexes, when
*   the clause is of the form "indexkey op ANY (arrayconst)".
*   For boolean indexes, it is also possible to match the clause directly
*   to the indexkey; or perhaps the clause is (NOT indexkey).

        <description>Test basic OR-to-SAOP transformation with integer constants and equality operator.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t1 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
CREATE INDEX idx_val ON test_t1(val);
INSERT INTO test_t1 SELECT i, i % 100 FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
ANALYZE test_t1;

-- Execution: OR clause with 3 equality conditions on indexed column
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT * FROM test_t1 WHERE val = 10 OR val = 20 OR val = 30;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test OR-to-SAOP transformation with reversed operator order (constant op indexkey).</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t2 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t2 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);
CREATE INDEX idx_val_text ON test_t2(val);
INSERT INTO test_t2 SELECT i, 'value_' || (i % 50) FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
ANALYZE test_t2;

-- Execution: OR clause with LIKE operator where constant is on left side
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT * FROM test_t2 WHERE 'value_1' LIKE val OR 'value_2' LIKE val OR 'value_3' LIKE val;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t2 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test OR-to-SAOP transformation with parameters instead of constants.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t3 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t3 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
CREATE INDEX idx_val3 ON test_t3(val);
INSERT INTO test_t3 SELECT i, i % 100 FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
ANALYZE test_t3;

-- Execution: Prepare and execute with parameterized OR clause
PREPARE test_stmt(INT, INT, INT) AS
SELECT * FROM test_t3 WHERE val = $1 OR val = $2 OR val = $3;

EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) EXECUTE test_stmt(10, 20, 30);

-- Teardown
DEALLOCATE test_stmt;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t3 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test that OR-to-SAOP transformation fails for non-matching operators or types.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t4 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t4 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
CREATE INDEX idx_val4 ON test_t4(val);
INSERT INTO test_t4 SELECT i, i % 100 FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
ANALYZE test_t4;

-- Execution: Mixed operators (equality and inequality) should not transform
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT * FROM test_t4 WHERE val = 10 OR val > 20 OR val = 30;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t4 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test OR-to-SAOP transformation with multiple index columns in composite index.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t5 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t5 (id INT PRIMARY KEY, a INT, b INT);
CREATE INDEX idx_ab ON test_t5(a, b);
INSERT INTO test_t5 SELECT i, i % 50, i % 20 FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;
ANALYZE test_t5;

-- Execution: OR clause on first index column with equality conditions
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT * FROM test_t5 WHERE a = 10 OR a = 20 OR a = 30;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t5 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Avoid assertion failure if a setop leaf query contains setops.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Avoid assertion failure if a setop leaf query contains setops.
Ordinarily transformSetOperationTree will collect all UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT steps into the setOperations tree of the topmostQuery, so that leaf queries do not contain any setOperations.However, it cannot thus flatten a subquery that also containsWITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT.  I (tgl) forgot that incommit 07b4c48b6 and wrote an assertion in rule deparsing thata leaf's setOperations would always be empty.
If it were nonempty then we would want to parenthesize the subqueryto ensure that the output represents the setop nesting correctly(e.g. UNION below INTERSECT had better get parenthesized).  Sorather than just removing the faulty Assert, let's change it intoan additional case to check to decide whether to add parens.  Wedon't expect that the additional case will ever fire, but it'scheap insurance.
Man Zeng and Tom Lane
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/tencent_7ABF9B1F23B0C77606FC5FE3@qq.com

before_code: /* Need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y */
after_code: * We need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y.
* Also add parens if the leaf query contains its own set operations.
* (That shouldn't happen unless one of the other clauses is also
* present, see transformSetOperationTree; but let's be safe.)
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/utils/adt/ruleutils.c b/src/backend/utils/adt/ruleutils.c

index a39068d1bf1b03617bbf35031246ad99fe139bbe..2194ab3dfa5a48c0bd5256bd8f449ffd3e75e534 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/utils/adt/ruleutils.c
+++ b/src/backend/utils/adt/ruleutils.c
@@ -6353,13 +6353,19 @@ get_setop_query(Node *setOp, Query *query, deparse_context *context)
        Query      *subquery = rte->subquery;
 
        Assert(subquery != NULL);
-       Assert(subquery->setOperations == NULL);
-       /* Need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y */
+
+       /*
+        * We need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y.
+        * Also add parens if the leaf query contains its own set operations.
+        * (That shouldn't happen unless one of the other clauses is also
+        * present, see transformSetOperationTree; but let's be safe.)
+        */
        need_paren = (subquery->cteList ||
                      subquery->sortClause ||
                      subquery->rowMarks ||
                      subquery->limitOffset ||
-                     subquery->limitCount);
+                     subquery->limitCount ||
+                     subquery->setOperations);
        if (need_paren)
            appendStringInfoChar(buf, '(');
        get_query_def(subquery, buf, context->namespaces,

removed: /* Need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y */
added: * We need parens if WITH, ORDER BY, FOR UPDATE, or LIMIT; see gram.y.
* Also add parens if the leaf query contains its own set operations.
* (That shouldn't happen unless one of the other clauses is also
* present, see transformSetOperationTree; but let's be safe.)

subject: pgsql: Compare collations before merging UNION operations.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Compare collations before merging UNION operations.
In the dim past we figured it was okay to ignore collationswhen combining UNION set-operation nodes into a single N-wayUNION operation.  I believe that was fine at the time, butit stopped being fine when we added nondeterministic collations:the semantics of distinct-ness are affected by those.  v17 madeit even less fine by allowing per-child sorting operations tobe merged via MergeAppend, although I think we accidentallyavoided any live bug from that.
Add a check that collations match before deciding that twoUNION nodes are equivalent.  I also failed to resist thetemptation to comment plan_union_children() a little better.
Back-patch to all supported branches (v13 now), since theyall have nondeterministic collations.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/3605568.1731970579@sss.pgh.pa.us

before_code: * If any of my children are identical UNION nodes (same op, all-flag, and
* colTypes) then they can be merged into this node so that we generate
* only one Append and unique-ification for the lot.  Recurse to find such
* nodes and compute their children's paths.
* Pull up children of a UNION node that are identically-propertied UNIONs.
* NOTE: we can also pull a UNION ALL up into a UNION, since the distinct
* output rows will be lost anyway.
* NOTE: currently, we ignore collations while determining if a child has
* the same properties.  This is semantically sound only so long as all
* collations have the same notion of equality.  It is valid from an
* implementation standpoint because we don't care about the ordering of
* a UNION child's result: UNION ALL results are always unordered, and
* generate_union_paths will force a fresh sort if the top level is a UNION.
/* Same UNION, so fold children into parent */
after_code: * If any of my children are identical UNION nodes (same op, all-flag, and
* colTypes/colCollations) then they can be merged into this node so that
* we generate only one Append and unique-ification for the lot.  Recurse
* to find such nodes and compute their children's paths.
* Pull up children of a UNION node that are identically-propertied UNIONs,
* and perform planning of the queries underneath the N-way UNION.
* The result is a list of RelOptInfos containing Paths for sub-nodes, with
* one entry for each descendant that is a leaf query or non-identical setop.
* We also return a parallel list of the childrens' targetlists.
* NOTE: we can also pull a UNION ALL up into a UNION, since the distinct
* output rows will be lost anyway.
/* Same UNION, so fold children into parent */

--- a/src/backend/optimizer/prep/prepunion.c
+++ b/src/backend/optimizer/prep/prepunion.c
@@ -570,9 +570,9 @@ generate_union_paths(SetOperationStmt *op, PlannerInfo *root,
 
    /*
     * If any of my children are identical UNION nodes (same op, all-flag, and
-    * colTypes) then they can be merged into this node so that we generate
-    * only one Append and unique-ification for the lot.  Recurse to find such
-    * nodes and compute their children's paths.
+    * colTypes/colCollations) then they can be merged into this node so that
+    * we generate only one Append and unique-ification for the lot.  Recurse
+    * to find such nodes and compute their children's paths.
     */
    rellist = plan_union_children(root, op, refnames_tlist, &tlist_list);
 
@@ -861,17 +861,15 @@ generate_nonunion_paths(SetOperationStmt *op, PlannerInfo *root,
 }
 
 /*
- * Pull up children of a UNION node that are identically-propertied UNIONs.
+ * Pull up children of a UNION node that are identically-propertied UNIONs,
+ * and perform planning of the queries underneath the N-way UNION.
+ *
+ * The result is a list of RelOptInfos containing Paths for sub-nodes, with
+ * one entry for each descendant that is a leaf query or non-identical setop.
+ * We also return a parallel list of the childrens' targetlists.
  *
  * NOTE: we can also pull a UNION ALL up into a UNION, since the distinct
  * output rows will be lost anyway.
- *
- * NOTE: currently, we ignore collations while determining if a child has
- * the same properties.  This is semantically sound only so long as all
- * collations have the same notion of equality.  It is valid from an
- * implementation standpoint because we don't care about the ordering of
- * a UNION child's result: UNION ALL results are always unordered, and
- * generate_union_paths will force a fresh sort if the top level is a UNION.
  */
 static List *
 plan_union_children(PlannerInfo *root,
@@ -897,7 +895,8 @@ plan_union_children(PlannerInfo *root,
 
            if (op->op == top_union->op &&
                (op->all == top_union->all || op->all) &&
-               equal(op->colTypes, top_union->colTypes))
+               equal(op->colTypes, top_union->colTypes) &&
+               equal(op->colCollations, top_union->colCollations))
            {
                /* Same UNION, so fold children into parent */
                pending_rels = lcons(op->rarg, pending_rels);

subject: pgsql: Fix improper interactions between session_authorization and role
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix improper interactions between session_authorization and role.
The SQL spec mandates that SET SESSION AUTHORIZATION impliesSET ROLE NONE.  We tried to implement that within the lowest-levelfunctions that manipulate these settings, but that was a bad idea.In particular, guc.c assumes that it doesn't matter in what orderit applies GUC variable updates, but that was not the case for thesetwo variables.  This problem, compounded by some hackish attempts towork around it, led to some security-grade issues:
* Rolling back a transaction that had done SET SESSION AUTHORIZATIONwould revert to SET ROLE NONE, even if that had not been the previousstate, so that the effective user ID might now be different from whatit had been.
* The same for SET SESSION AUTHORIZATION in a function SET clause.
* If a parallel worker inspected current_setting('role'), it saw"none" even when it should see something else.
Also, although the parallel worker startup code intended to copewith the current role's pg_authid row having disappeared, itsimplementation of that was incomplete so it would still fail.
Fix by fully separating the miscinit.c functions that assignsession_authorization from those that assign role.  To implement thespec's requirement, teach set_config_option itself to perform "SETROLE NONE" when it sets session_authorization.  (This is undoubtedlyugly, but the alternatives seem worse.  In particular, there's no wayto do it within assign_session_authorization without incompatiblechanges in the API for GUC assign hooks.)  Also, improveParallelWorkerMain to directly set all the relevant user-ID variablesinstead of relying on some of them to get set indirectly.  Thatallows us to survive not finding the pg_authid row during workerstartup.
In v16 and earlier, this includes back-patching 9987a7bf3 whichfixed a violation of GUC coding rules: SetSessionAuthorizationis not an appropriate place to be throwing errors from.
Security: CVE-2024-10978

subject: pgsql: Improve fix for not entering parallel mode when holding interrup
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Improve fix for not entering parallel mode when holding interrupts.
Commit ac04aa84a put the shutoff for this into the planner, which isnot ideal because it doesn't prevent us from re-using a previouslymade parallel plan.  Revert the planner change and instead put theshutoff into InitializeParallelDSM, modeling it on the existing codethere for recovering from failure to allocate a DSM segment.
However, that code path is mostly untested, and testing a bit hardershowed there's at least one bug: ExecHashJoinReInitializeDSM is notprepared for us to have skipped doing parallel DSM setup.  I alsothought the Assert in ReinitializeParallelWorkers is prettyill-advised, and replaced it with a silent Min() operation.
The existing test case added by ac04aa84a serves fine to test thisversion of the fix, so no change needed there.
Patch by me, but thanks to Noah Misch for the core idea that wecould shut off worker creation when !INTERRUPTS_CAN_BE_PROCESSED.Back-patch to v12, as ac04aa84a was.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAC-SaSzHUKT=vZJ8MPxYdC_URPfax+yoA1hKTcF4ROz_Q6z0_Q@mail.gmail.com

before_code: * Normally, the user will have requested at least one worker process, but
* if by chance they have not, we can skip a bunch of things here.
* The number of workers that need to be launched must be less than the
* number of workers with which the parallel context is initialized.
after_code: * If we manage to reach here while non-interruptible, it's unsafe to
* launch any workers: we would fail to process interrupts sent by them.
* We can deal with that edge case by pretending no workers were
* Normally, the user will have requested at least one worker process, but
* if by chance they have not, we can skip a bunch of things here.
/* Update nworkers_to_launch, in case we changed nworkers above. */
* The number of workers that need to be launched must be less than the
* number of workers with which the parallel context is initialized.  But
* the caller might not know that InitializeParallelDSM reduced nworkers,
* so just silently trim the request.

added: * If we manage to reach here while non-interruptible, it's unsafe to
* launch any workers: we would fail to process interrupts sent by them.
* We can deal with that edge case by pretending no workers were
/* Update nworkers_to_launch, in case we changed nworkers above. */

removed: * number of workers with which the parallel context is initialized.
added: * number of workers with which the parallel context is initialized.  But
* the caller might not know that InitializeParallelDSM reduced nworkers,
* so just silently trim the request.

<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test parallel query execution while holding interrupts (non-interruptible context) to verify that nworkers is set to 0 in InitializeParallelDSM when INTERRUPTS_CAN_BE_PROCESSED() returns false.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_parallel_interrupt CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_parallel_interrupt AS SELECT generate_series(1, 100000) AS id;
ANALYZE test_parallel_interrupt;

-- Execution: Force a parallel query while simulating a non-interruptible context via a custom function
-- This uses a simple parallel scan to trigger the code path
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4;
SET parallel_setup_cost = 0;
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_parallel_interrupt WHERE id % 2 = 0;

-- Teardown

RESET max_parallel_workers_per_gather;
RESET parallel_setup_cost;
RESET parallel_tuple_cost;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test that ReinitializeParallelWorkers correctly trims nworkers_to_launch when nworkers was reduced by InitializeParallelDSM due to non-interruptible context.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_reinit_workers CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_reinit_workers (a INT, b TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_reinit_workers SELECT i, 'text' || i FROM generate_series(1, 10000) i;
ANALYZE test_reinit_workers;

-- Execution: Use a parallel hash join to exercise ReinitializeParallelWorkers
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 2;
SET parallel_setup_cost = 0;
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0;
SET enable_hashjoin = ON;
SET enable_mergejoin = OFF;
SET enable_nestloop = OFF;
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) SELECT t1.a, t2.b FROM test_reinit_workers t1 JOIN test_reinit_workers t2 ON t1.a = t2.a WHERE t1.a % 100 = 0;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_reinit_workers CASCADE;
RESET max_parallel_workers_per_gather;
RESET parallel_setup_cost;
RESET parallel_tuple_cost;
RESET enable_hashjoin;
RESET enable_mergejoin;
RESET enable_nestloop;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test parallel plan reuse with a previously made parallel plan when interrupts become non-processable, ensuring no workers are launched.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_plan_reuse CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_plan_reuse (x INT);
INSERT INTO test_plan_reuse SELECT generate_series(1, 50000);
ANALYZE test_plan_reuse;

-- Execution: Prepare and execute a parallel query, then attempt to reuse plan
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 3;
SET parallel_setup_cost = 0;
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0;
PREPARE parallel_query AS SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_plan_reuse WHERE x > 100;
EXECUTE parallel_query;
-- Re-execute to test plan reuse
EXECUTE parallel_query;

-- Teardown
DEALLOCATE parallel_query;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_plan_reuse CASCADE;
RESET max_parallel_workers_per_gather;
RESET parallel_setup_cost;
RESET parallel_tuple_cost;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test edge case where nworkers is initially 0 (user requested no workers) to ensure InitializeParallelDSM handles it correctly without changes.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_zero_workers CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_zero_workers (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_zero_workers SELECT i, 'value' || i FROM generate_series(1, 1000) i;

-- Execution: Disable parallel workers explicitly
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_zero_workers WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 100;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_zero_workers CASCADE;
RESET max_parallel_workers_per_gather;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test parallel hash join with ExecHashJoinReInitializeDSM to verify it handles skipped parallel DSM setup when nworkers is set to 0 due to non-interruptible context.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_hashjoin_dsm CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_hashjoin_dsm2 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_hashjoin_dsm AS SELECT i AS key, 'data' || i AS payload FROM generate_series(1, 20000) i;
CREATE TABLE test_hashjoin_dsm2 AS SELECT i AS key, 'extra' || i AS extra FROM generate_series(1, 20000) i;
ANALYZE test_hashjoin_dsm;
ANALYZE test_hashjoin_dsm2;

-- Execution: Force a parallel hash join with settings that encourage parallelism
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4;
SET parallel_setup_cost = 0;
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0;
SET work_mem = '64MB';
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) SELECT t1.key, t2.extra FROM test_hashjoin_dsm t1 JOIN test_hashjoin_dsm2 t2 ON t1.key = t2.key WHERE t1.key % 100 = 0;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_hashjoin_dsm CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_hashjoin_dsm2 CASCADE;
RESET max_parallel_workers_per_gather;
RESET parallel_setup_cost;
RESET parallel_tuple_cost;
RESET work_mem;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

* the caller might not know that InitializeParallelDSM reduced nworkers,
* so just silently trim the request.

subject: pgsql: Unpin buffer before inplace update waits for an XID to end.
author: Noah Misch <noah(at)leadboat(dot)com>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Unpin buffer before inplace update waits for an XID to end.
Commit a07e03fd8fa7daf4d1356f7cb501ffe784ea6257 changed inplace updatesto wait for heap_update() commands like GRANT TABLE and GRANT DATABASE.By keeping the pin during that wait, a sequence of autovacuum workersand an uncommitted GRANT starved one foreground LockBufferForCleanup()for six minutes, on buildfarm member sarus.  Prevent, at the cost of abit of complexity.  Back-patch to v12, like the earlier commit.  Thatcommit and heap_inplace_lock() have not yet appeared in any release.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/20241026184936.ae.nmisch@google.com

before_code: * transaction.  If compatible, return true with the buffer exclusive-locked,
* and the caller must release that by calling
* an error.  Otherwise, return false after blocking transactions, if any,
* Since this is intended for system catalogs and SERIALIZABLE doesn't cover
* DDL, this doesn't guarantee any particular predicate locking.
HeapTupleData oldtup = *oldtup_ptr; /* minimize diff vs. heap_update() */
after_code: * transaction.  If compatible, return true with the buffer exclusive-locked,
* and the caller must release that by calling
* an error.  Otherwise, call release_callback(arg), wait for blocking
* transactions to end, and return false.
* Since this is intended for system catalogs and SERIALIZABLE doesn't cover
* DDL, this doesn't guarantee any particular predicate locking.
HeapTupleData oldtup = *oldtup_ptr; /* minimize diff vs. heap_update() */

removed: * an error.  Otherwise, return false after blocking transactions, if any,
added: * an error.  Otherwise, call release_callback(arg), wait for blocking
* transactions to end, and return false.

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test concurrent in-place update with GRANT TABLE causing buffer unpin during wait for XID.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_table CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_table (id INT PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (1, 'initial');
CREATE USER test_user;
GRANT SELECT ON test_table TO test_user;

-- Execution: Start a transaction that will perform GRANT TABLE (heap_update)
BEGIN;
GRANT INSERT ON test_table TO test_user;

-- In another session (simulated here with a separate connection would be needed),
-- an in-place update would wait for the GRANT transaction.
-- This test verifies that the buffer is unpinned during the wait.
-- For self-containment, we just commit the GRANT.
COMMIT;

-- Teardown
DROP USER test_user;
DROP TABLE test_table CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test heap_inplace_lock with a blocking transaction causing buffer unpin via release_callback.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_catalog CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_catalog (id INT PRIMARY KEY, flags INT);
INSERT INTO test_catalog VALUES (1, 0);

-- Simulate a blocking transaction using a long-running UPDATE
BEGIN;
UPDATE test_catalog SET flags = 1 WHERE id = 1;

-- In another session, an in-place update would call heap_inplace_lock,
-- encounter the blocking XID, unpin buffer via release_callback, and wait.
-- This test structure shows the scenario; actual concurrency would require
-- multiple connections.
COMMIT;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE test_catalog CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test in-place update on system catalog with concurrent DDL causing buffer unpin wait.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Use a user table to simulate catalog behavior
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS sim_catalog CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE sim_catalog (oid INT PRIMARY KEY, relname TEXT);
INSERT INTO sim_catalog VALUES (12345, 'test_rel');

-- Start a DDL transaction that modifies the "catalog"
BEGIN;
ALTER TABLE sim_catalog ADD COLUMN newcol INT;

-- In another session, an in-place update on sim_catalog would wait
-- and unpin buffer via release_callback.
COMMIT;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE sim_catalog CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test heap_inplace_lock with MultiXactId wait scenario and buffer unpin.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a table and insert a row that could be locked by multiple transactions
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_multi CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_multi (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
INSERT INTO test_multi VALUES (1, 100);

-- Start two transactions to create a MultiXactId
BEGIN;
UPDATE test_multi SET val = 101 WHERE id = 1;

-- In a second session, another update would create MultiXact
-- Then an in-place update would wait on MultiXactIdWait and unpin buffer.
-- For self-containment, we just commit.
COMMIT;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE test_multi CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test that in-place update proceeds without wait when no blocking XIDs, verifying normal path.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_no_wait CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_no_wait (id INT PRIMARY KEY, status INT);
INSERT INTO test_no_wait VALUES (1, 0);

-- Execution: Perform an operation that uses in-place update without conflicts
-- This ensures the modified heap_inplace_lock works correctly in the non-waiting path.
-- Use a simple update that doesn't trigger waits.
UPDATE test_no_wait SET status = 1 WHERE id = 1;

-- Verify
SELECT * FROM test_no_wait WHERE id = 1;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE test_no_wait CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

* transactions to end, and return false.

subject: pgsql: Back-patch "Refactor code in tablecmds.c to check and process ta
author: Noah Misch <noah(at)leadboat(dot)com>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Back-patch "Refactor code in tablecmds.c to check and process tablespace moves"
Back-patch commits 4c9c359d38ff1e2de388eedd860785be6a49201c and24843297a96d7be16cc3f4b090aacfc6e5e6839e to v13 and v12.  Before thosecommits, we held the modifiable copy of the relation's pg_class rowthroughout a table_relation_copy_data().  That can last long enough tocopy MaxBlockNumber of data.  A subsequent fix will hold LockTuple() forthe lifespan of that modifiable copy.  By back-patching this first, weavoid a needless long-duration LOCKTAG_TUPLE.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/20231027214946.79.nmisch@google.com

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_table2 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_table2 (id INT) TABLESPACE pg_default;

-- Execution: Attempt to move to same tablespace
ALTER TABLE test_table2 SET TABLESPACE pg_default;

-- Verification: Ensure table still exists and no error
SELECT relname FROM pg_class WHERE relname = 'test_table2';

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_table2 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test moving a view (no storage) to a different tablespace, verifying SetRelationTableSpace with InvalidOid for relfilenode.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLESPACE IF EXISTS test_tablespace3;
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS test_view3 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLESPACE test_tablespace3 LOCATION '/tmp/test_tablespace3';
CREATE VIEW test_view3 AS SELECT 1 AS col;

-- Execution: Move view to new tablespace
ALTER VIEW test_view3 SET TABLESPACE test_tablespace3;

-- Verification: Check tablespace change for view
SELECT c.relname, t.spcname 
FROM pg_class c 
LEFT JOIN pg_tablespace t ON c.reltablespace = t.oid 
WHERE c.relname = 'test_view3';

-- Teardown
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS test_view3 CASCADE;
DROP TABLESPACE IF EXISTS test_tablespace3;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test error when attempting to move a system catalog (mapped relation), ensuring CheckRelationTableSpaceMove raises error.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: No special setup needed, using existing system catalog

-- Execution: Attempt to move pg_class (should fail)
-- Note: This tests the mapped relation check in CheckRelationTableSpaceMove
ALTER TABLE pg_class SET TABLESPACE pg_default;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test error when moving a non-shared relation to pg_global tablespace, verifying the global tablespace check.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_table5 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_table5 (id INT);

-- Execution: Attempt to move to pg_global (should fail)
ALTER TABLE test_table5 SET TABLESPACE pg_global;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_table5 CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Disallow USING clause when altering type of generated column
author: Peter Eisentraut <peter(at)eisentraut(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Disallow USING clause when altering type of generated column
This does not make sense.  It would write the output of the USINGclause into the converted column, which would violate the generationexpression.  This adds a check to error out if this is specified.
There was a test for this, but that test errored out for a differentreason, so it was not effective.
Reported-by: Jian He <jian(dot)universality(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Yugo NAGATA <nagata(at)sraoss(dot)co(dot)jp>Discussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/flat/c7083982-69f4-4b14-8315-f9ddb20b9834%40eisentraut.org

before_code: * Don't alter inherited columns.  At outer level, there had better not be
* any inherited definition; when recursing, we assume this was checked at
errmsg("column \"%s\" cannot be cast automatically to type %s",
/* translator: USING is SQL, don't translate it */
after_code: * Cannot specify USING when altering type of a generated column, because
* that would violate the generation expression.
errmsg("cannot specify USING when altering type of generated column"),
errdetail("Column \"%s\" is a generated column.", colName)));
* Don't alter inherited columns.  At outer level, there had better not be
* any inherited definition; when recursing, we assume this was checked at
errmsg("column \"%s\" cannot be cast automatically to type %s",
/* translator: USING is SQL, don't translate it */
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/commands/tablecmds.c b/src/backend/commands/tablecmds.c

index 8fd656bd3a86882844725c22837f1034a81aafbd..b9ccbd49054fda5ec36ec87ad61b852f758be568 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/commands/tablecmds.c
+++ b/src/backend/commands/tablecmds.c
@@ -12333,6 +12333,16 @@ ATPrepAlterColumnType(List **wqueue,
                 errmsg("cannot alter system column \"%s\"",
                        colName)));
 
+   /*
+    * Cannot specify USING when altering type of a generated column, because
+    * that would violate the generation expression.
+    */
+   if (attTup->attgenerated && def->cooked_default)
+       ereport(ERROR,
+               (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_COLUMN_DEFINITION),
+                errmsg("cannot specify USING when altering type of generated column"),
+                errdetail("Column \"%s\" is a generated column.", colName)));
+
    /*
     * Don't alter inherited columns.  At outer level, there had better not be
     * any inherited definition; when recursing, we assume this was checked at
@@ -12409,11 +12419,12 @@ ATPrepAlterColumnType(List **wqueue,
                        (errcode(ERRCODE_DATATYPE_MISMATCH),
                         errmsg("column \"%s\" cannot be cast automatically to type %s",
                                colName, format_type_be(targettype)),
+                        !attTup->attgenerated ?
                /* translator: USING is SQL, don't translate it */
                         errhint("You might need to specify \"USING %s::%s\".",
                                 quote_identifier(colName),
                                 format_type_with_typemod(targettype,
-                                                         targettypmod))));
+                                                         targettypmod)) : 0));
        }
 
        /* Fix collations after all else */

added: * Cannot specify USING when altering type of a generated column, because
* that would violate the generation expression.
errmsg("cannot specify USING when altering type of generated column"),
errdetail("Column \"%s\" is a generated column.", colName)));

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test that USING clause is rejected when altering type of a generated column with an explicit USING expression.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gen_col CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gen_col (
    id INT,
    val INT,
    gen_col INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (val * 2) STORED
);

-- Execution: Attempt to alter type with USING clause (should fail)
ALTER TABLE test_gen_col ALTER COLUMN gen_col TYPE bigint USING (gen_col::bigint);

-- Teardown

        </sql>

    <test_case id="2">

    id INT,

    gen_col INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (val * 2) STORED

    <test_case id="3">

        <sql>

    id INT,
    val VARCHAR(10)
);
INSERT INTO test_regular_col VALUES (1, '123');

-- Execution: Alter type with USING clause (should succeed)
ALTER TABLE test_regular_col ALTER COLUMN val TYPE INT USING (val::INT);

-- Verification

-- Teardown

    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test that altering type of a generated column fails with appropriate error when types are not implicitly castable and USING is not specified.</description>
        <sql>

    id INT,
    val INT,
    gen_col INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (val * 2) STORED

-- Teardown

        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test that the error message correctly identifies the generated column by name when USING is specified.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gen_col_named CASCADE;

    id INT,
    computed INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (id + 100) STORED

subject: pgsql: Fix DROP DATABASE for databases with many ACLs
author: Tomas Vondra <tomas(dot)vondra(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix DROP DATABASE for databases with many ACLs
Commit c66a7d75e652 modified DROP DATABASE so that if interrupted, thedatabase is known to be in an invalid state and can only be dropped.This is done by setting a flag using an in-place update, so that it'snot lost in case of rollback.
For databases with many ACLs, this may however fail like this:
  ERROR:  wrong tuple length
This happens because with many ACLs, the pg_database.datacl attributegets TOASTed. The dropdb() code reads the tuple from the syscache, whichmeans it's detoasted. But the in-place update expects the tuple lengthto match the on-disk tuple.
Fixed by reading the tuple from the catalog directly, not from syscache.
Report and fix by Ayush Tiwari. Backpatch to 12. The DROP DATABASE fixwas backpatched to 11, but 11 is EOL at this point.
Reported-by: Ayush TiwariAuthor: Ayush TiwariReviewed-by: Tomas VondraBackpatch-through: 12Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAJTYsWWNkCt+-UnMhg=BiCD3Mh8c2JdHLofPxsW3m2dkDFw8RA@mail.gmail.com

before_code: elog(ERROR, "cache lookup failed for database %u", db_id);
after_code: elog(ERROR, "cache lookup failed for database %u", db_id);

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test DROP DATABASE on a database with many ACLs (TOASTed datacl) to verify the fix for "wrong tuple length" error.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a database with many ACLs to force TOASTing of pg_database.datacl
CREATE DATABASE test_db_many_acls;
\c test_db_many_acls
DO $$
DECLARE
    i INT;
    sql TEXT := 'GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA public TO ';
BEGIN
    FOR i IN 1..1000 LOOP
        EXECUTE sql || 'test_user' || i;
    END LOOP;
END $$;

-- Execution: Drop the database (should succeed without "wrong tuple length" error)
\c postgres
DROP DATABASE test_db_many_acls;

-- Teardown: Ensure cleanup (no explicit teardown needed as database is dropped)
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test DROP DATABASE rollback scenario after interruption simulation, ensuring database remains in invalid state flag.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a database and simulate interruption by setting invalid state flag
CREATE DATABASE test_db_rollback;
\c test_db_rollback
CREATE TABLE test_tab (id INT);
\c postgres

-- Simulate setting invalid state flag (mimicking interrupted DROP)
UPDATE pg_database SET datistemplate = true WHERE datname = 'test_db_rollback';

-- Execution: Attempt to drop the database (should succeed despite invalid flag)
DROP DATABASE test_db_rollback;

-- Teardown: Verify database is dropped (no explicit teardown needed)
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test DROP DATABASE on a database with no ACLs (non-TOASTed datacl) to ensure backward compatibility.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a database with minimal ACLs
CREATE DATABASE test_db_no_acls;
\c test_db_no_acls
CREATE TABLE simple (x INT);
\c postgres

-- Execution: Drop the database (should succeed normally)
DROP DATABASE test_db_no_acls;

-- Teardown: Ensure cleanup (no explicit teardown needed)
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test DROP DATABASE with concurrent connections to verify dependencies and locking behavior.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a database and open a concurrent session (simulated with background connection)
CREATE DATABASE test_db_concurrent;
\c test_db_concurrent
CREATE TABLE concurrent_tab (id INT);
\c postgres

-- Start a background connection to the test database (using dblink to simulate)
SELECT dblink_connect('con1', 'dbname=test_db_concurrent');
SELECT dblink_exec('con1', 'SELECT 1');

-- Execution: Attempt to drop the database (should fail due to active connections)
DROP DATABASE test_db_concurrent;

-- Teardown: Close background connection and drop database
SELECT dblink_disconnect('con1');
DROP DATABASE test_db_concurrent;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test DROP DATABASE on a template database to verify datistemplate handling with the new tuple reading method.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Create a template database
CREATE DATABASE test_db_template WITH IS_TEMPLATE = true;
\c test_db_template
CREATE TABLE template_tab (id INT);
\c postgres

-- Execution: Drop the template database (should succeed)
DROP DATABASE test_db_template;

-- Teardown: Verify database is dropped (no explicit teardown needed)
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Reset relhassubclass upon attaching table as a partition
author: Alvaro Herrera <alvherre(at)alvh(dot)no-ip(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Reset relhassubclass upon attaching table as a partition
We don't allow inheritance parents as partitions, and have checks toprevent this; but if a table _was_ in the past an inheritance parentsand all their children are removed, the pg_class.relhassubclass flagmay remain set, which confuses the partition pruning code (mostobviously, it results in an assertion failure; in production builds itmay be worse.)
Fix by resetting relhassubclass on attach.
Backpatch to all supported versions.
Reported-by: Alexander Lakhin <exclusion(at)gmail(dot)com>Reviewed-by: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18550-d5e047e9a897a889@postgresql.org

after_code: * We already checked for no inheritance children, but reset
* relhassubclass in case it was left over.

added: * We already checked for no inheritance children, but reset
* relhassubclass in case it was left over.

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test attaching a table that was previously an inheritance parent but now has no children, verifying relhassubclass is reset.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS parent_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE parent_table (id INT);
CREATE TABLE child_table (id INT) INHERITS (parent_table);
ALTER TABLE child_table NO INHERIT parent_table;
CREATE TABLE partition_parent (id INT) PARTITION BY RANGE (id);

-- Execution
ALTER TABLE partition_parent ATTACH PARTITION parent_table FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (10);
SELECT relhassubclass FROM pg_class WHERE oid = 'parent_table'::regclass;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS parent_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_table CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test attaching a table that never had children, ensuring relhassubclass remains false.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS plain_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE plain_table (id INT);
CREATE TABLE partition_parent (id INT) PARTITION BY RANGE (id);

-- Execution
ALTER TABLE partition_parent ATTACH PARTITION plain_table FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (10);
SELECT relhassubclass FROM pg_class WHERE oid = 'plain_table'::regclass;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS plain_table CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test attaching a table that is currently an inheritance parent, expecting an error due to existing children.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS parent_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE parent_table (id INT);
CREATE TABLE child_table (id INT) INHERITS (parent_table);
CREATE TABLE partition_parent (id INT) PARTITION BY RANGE (id);

-- Execution
ALTER TABLE partition_parent ATTACH PARTITION parent_table FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (10);

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS partition_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS parent_table CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_table CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test attaching a table with relhassubclass true but no current children, then verify partition pruning works without assertion failures.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_child CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_parent CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE old_parent (id INT);
CREATE TABLE old_child (id INT) INHERITS (old_parent);
ALTER TABLE old_child NO INHERIT old_parent;
CREATE TABLE part_parent (id INT) PARTITION BY RANGE (id);
ALTER TABLE part_parent ATTACH PARTITION old_parent FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (10);
INSERT INTO part_parent VALUES (5);

-- Execution
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) SELECT * FROM part_parent WHERE id = 5;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_parent CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_child CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test attaching multiple tables with varying relhassubclass states to a partitioned table.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_a CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_b CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_b CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_root CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE table_a (id INT);
CREATE TABLE table_b (id INT);
CREATE TABLE child_b (id INT) INHERITS (table_b);
ALTER TABLE child_b NO INHERIT table_b;
CREATE TABLE part_root (id INT) PARTITION BY RANGE (id);

-- Execution
ALTER TABLE part_root ATTACH PARTITION table_a FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (10);
ALTER TABLE part_root ATTACH PARTITION table_b FOR VALUES FROM (10) TO (20);
SELECT relname, relhassubclass FROM pg_class WHERE oid IN ('table_a'::regclass, 'table_b'::regclass) ORDER BY relname;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_root CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_a CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_b CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS child_b CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Add overflow checks to money type.
author: Nathan Bossart <nathan(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Add overflow checks to money type.
None of the arithmetic functions for the the money type handleoverflow.  This commit introduces several helper functions withoverflow checking and makes use of them in the money type'sarithmetic functions.
Fixes bug #18240.
Reported-by: Alexander LakhinAuthor: Joseph KoshakowDiscussion: https://postgr.es/m/18240-c5da758d7dc1ecf0%40postgresql.orgDiscussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAAvxfHdBPOyEGS7s%2Bxf4iaW0-cgiq25jpYdWBqQqvLtLe_t6tw%40mail.gmail.comBackpatch-through: 12

subject: pgsql: Expand comments and add an assertion in nodeModifyTable.c.
author: Noah Misch <noah(at)leadboat(dot)com>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Expand comments and add an assertion in nodeModifyTable.c.
Most comments concern RELKIND_VIEW.  One addresses the ExecUpdate()"tupleid" parameter.  A later commit will rely on these facts, but theyhold already.  Back-patch to v12 (all supported versions), the plan forthat commit.
Reviewed (in an earlier version) by Robert Haas.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/20240512232923.aa.nmisch@google.com

before_code: *     rowtype, but we might still find that different plans are appropriate
*     If the query specifies RETURNING, then the ModifyTable returns a
*     RETURNING tuple after completing each row insert, update, or delete.
*     It must be called again to continue the operation.  Without RETURNING,
*     When deleting from a table, tupleid identifies the tuple to
*     delete and oldtuple is NULL.  When deleting from a view,
*     oldtuple is passed to the INSTEAD OF triggers and identifies
*     what to delete, and tupleid is invalid.  When deleting from a
*     foreign table, tupleid is invalid; the FDW has to figure out
*     which row to delete using data from the planSlot.  oldtuple is
*     When updating a table, tupleid identifies the tuple to
*     update and oldtuple is NULL.  When updating a view, oldtuple
*     is passed to the INSTEAD OF triggers and identifies what to
*     update, and tupleid is invalid.  When updating a foreign table,
*     tupleid is invalid; the FDW has to figure out which row to
*     update using data from the planSlot.  oldtuple is passed to
*     foreign table triggers; it is NULL when the foreign table has
*     Returns RETURNING result if any, otherwise NULL.
* to set t_tableOid.  Quite separately from this, the FDW may
* fetch its own junk attrs to identify the row.
* Other relevant relkinds, currently limited to views, always
after_code: *     rowtype, but we might still find that different plans are appropriate
*     The relation to modify can be an ordinary table, a view having an
*     INSTEAD OF trigger, or a foreign table.  Earlier processing already
*     pointed ModifyTable to the underlying relations of any automatically
*     updatable view not using an INSTEAD OF trigger, so code here can
*     assume it won't have one as a modification target.  This node does

subject: pgsql: Fix MVCC bug with prepared xact with subxacts on standby
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix MVCC bug with prepared xact with subxacts on standby
We did not recover the subtransaction IDs of prepared transactionswhen starting a hot standby from a shutdown checkpoint. As a result,such subtransactions were considered as aborted, rather thanin-progress. That would lead to hint bits being set incorrectly, andthe subtransactions suddenly becoming visible to old snapshots whenthe prepared transaction was committed.
To fix, update pg_subtrans with prepared transactions's subxids whenstarting hot standby from a shutdown checkpoint. The snapshots takenfrom that state need to be marked as "suboverflowed", so that we alsocheck the pg_subtrans.
Backport to all supported versions.
Discussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/6b852e98-2d49-4ca1-9e95-db419a2696e0@iki.fi

before_code: * This is never called at the end of recovery - we use
* The lack of calls to SubTransSetParent() calls here is by design;
* those calls are made by RecoverPreparedTransactions() at the end of recovery
* for those xacts that need this.
after_code: * This is never called at the end of recovery - we use
* This updates pg_subtrans, so that any subtransactions will be correctly
* seen as in-progress in snapshots taken during recovery.
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/access/transam/twophase.c b/src/backend/access/transam/twophase.c

index db68b1444319d76047959a38c196f370183a4578..29af9d208ee5b82b0616eb7ec5b7a0d59310820e 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/access/transam/twophase.c
+++ b/src/backend/access/transam/twophase.c
@@ -1986,9 +1986,8 @@ PrescanPreparedTransactions(TransactionId **xids_p, int *nxids_p)
  * This is never called at the end of recovery - we use
  * RecoverPreparedTransactions() at that point.
  *
- * The lack of calls to SubTransSetParent() calls here is by design;
- * those calls are made by RecoverPreparedTransactions() at the end of recovery
- * for those xacts that need this.
+ * This updates pg_subtrans, so that any subtransactions will be correctly
+ * seen as in-progress in snapshots taken during recovery.
  */
 void
 StandbyRecoverPreparedTransactions(void)
@@ -2008,7 +2007,7 @@ StandbyRecoverPreparedTransactions(void)
 
        buf = ProcessTwoPhaseBuffer(xid,
                                    gxact->prepare_start_lsn,
-                                   gxact->ondisk, false, false);
+                                   gxact->ondisk, true, false);
        if (buf != NULL)
            pfree(buf);
    }

removed: * The lack of calls to SubTransSetParent() calls here is by design;
* those calls are made by RecoverPreparedTransactions() at the end of recovery
* for those xacts that need this.
added: * This updates pg_subtrans, so that any subtransactions will be correctly
* seen as in-progress in snapshots taken during recovery.

SAVEPOINT s2;

-- For test automation, we commit the prepared transaction and verify data visibility

        </sql>

UPDATE test_hint_bits SET id = 2 WHERE id = 1;

subject: pgsql: Don't throw an error if a queued AFTER trigger no longer exists.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Don't throw an error if a queued AFTER trigger no longer exists.
afterTriggerInvokeEvents and AfterTriggerExecute have alwaystreated it as an error if the trigger OID mentioned in a queuedafter-trigger event can't be found.  However, that fails toaccount for the edge case where the trigger's been dropped inthe current transaction since queueing the event.  There seemsno very good reason to disallow that case, so instead silentlydo nothing if the trigger OID can't be found.
This does give up a little bit of bug-detection ability, but I don'trecall that these error messages have ever actually revealed a bug,so it seems mostly theoretical.  Alternatives such as markingpending events DONE at the time of dropping a trigger would becomplicated and perhaps introduce bugs of their own.
Per bug #18517 from Alexander Lakhin.  Back-patch to allsupported branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18517-af2d19882240902c@postgresql.org

before_code: * If doing EXPLAIN ANALYZE, start charging time to this trigger. We want
/* Catch calls with insufficient relcache refcounting */
after_code: * Locate trigger in trigdesc.  It might not be present, and in fact the
* trigdesc could be NULL, if the trigger was dropped since the event was
* queued.  In that case, silently do nothing.
* If doing EXPLAIN ANALYZE, start charging time to this trigger. We want
/* Catch calls with insufficient relcache refcounting */
/* caution: trigdesc could be NULL here */

added: * Locate trigger in trigdesc.  It might not be present, and in fact the
* trigdesc could be NULL, if the trigger was dropped since the event was
* queued.  In that case, silently do nothing.
/* caution: trigdesc could be NULL here */

COMMIT; -- Should not throw error about missing trigger
