END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
CREATE TRIGGER test_trigger1 AFTER INSERT ON test_t1 FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION test_trigger_func1();
CREATE TRIGGER test_trigger2 AFTER INSERT ON test_t1 FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION test_trigger_func2();

-- Execution
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_t1 VALUES (1); -- Queues both AFTER trigger events
DROP TRIGGER test_trigger1 ON test_t1; -- Drop first trigger
COMMIT; -- Should fire only trigger2 without error

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_trigger_func1() CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_trigger_func2() CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test dropping an AFTER trigger in a subtransaction (SAVEPOINT) after queuing, then rolling back to the savepoint.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t1 (id INT);
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION test_trigger_func() RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
CREATE TRIGGER test_trigger AFTER INSERT ON test_t1 FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION test_trigger_func();

-- Execution
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_t1 VALUES (1); -- Queues AFTER trigger event
SAVEPOINT s1;
DROP TRIGGER test_trigger ON test_t1;
ROLLBACK TO s1; -- Trigger should still exist
COMMIT; -- Should fire trigger normally

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_trigger_func() CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test dropping an AFTER trigger on a table with INSTEAD OF trigger on a view, ensuring no error when trigger is missing.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS test_v1 CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_t1 (id INT);
CREATE VIEW test_v1 AS SELECT * FROM test_t1;
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION test_trigger_func() RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
CREATE TRIGGER test_trigger AFTER INSERT ON test_t1 FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION test_trigger_func();

-- Execution: Use view to queue trigger, then drop trigger
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_v1 VALUES (1); -- Queues AFTER trigger event on base table
DROP TRIGGER test_trigger ON test_t1;
COMMIT; -- Should not throw error

-- Teardown
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS test_v1 CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_t1 CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS test_trigger_func() CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * Locate trigger in trigdesc.  It might not be present, and in fact the
* trigdesc could be NULL, if the trigger was dropped since the event was
* queued.  In that case, silently do nothing.

subject: pgsql: Clean out column-level pg_init_privs entries when dropping table
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Clean out column-level pg_init_privs entries when dropping tables.
DeleteInitPrivs did not get the memo about how, when dropping awhole object (with subid == 0), you should drop entries relatingto its sub-objects too.  This is visible in the test_pg_dump testcase if one drops the extension at the end: the entry for        GRANT SELECT(col1) ON regress_pg_dump_table TO public;was still present in pg_init_privs afterwards, although it waspointing to a dangling table OID.
Noted while fooling with a fix for REASSIGN OWNED for pg_init_privsentries.  This bug is aboriginal in the pg_init_privs featurethough, and there seems no reason not to back-patch the fix.

before_code: * Delete any comments, security labels, or initial privileges associated
* with this object.  (This is a convenient place to do these things,
* rather than having every object type know to do it.)
after_code: * Delete any comments, security labels, or initial privileges associated
* with this object.  (This is a convenient place to do these things,
* rather than having every object type know to do it.)  As above, all
* these functions must remove records for sub-objects too if the subid is

removed: * rather than having every object type know to do it.)
added: * rather than having every object type know to do it.)  As above, all
* these functions must remove records for sub-objects too if the subid is

    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test that dropping a table with column-level initial privileges removes all related pg_init_privs entries (subid=0 case).</description>

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS regress_pg_dump_table CASCADE;

GRANT SELECT(col1) ON regress_pg_dump_table TO PUBLIC;
REVOKE ALL ON regress_pg_dump_table FROM PUBLIC;

-- Verification: Check pg_init_privs for any dangling entries

WHERE classid = 'pg_class'::regclass 
AND objid = (SELECT oid FROM pg_class WHERE relname = 'regress_pg_dump_table');

-- Teardown

        </sql>

    <test_case id="2">

WHERE objid = 'test_col_privs'::regclass ORDER BY objsubid;

-- Teardown

        </sql>

WHERE objid = 'multi_col_grants'::regclass;

WHERE objid = 'multi_col_grants'::regclass;

-- Teardown

CREATE ROLE test_owner2;
CREATE TABLE owned_table (col1 INT, col2 INT);
ALTER TABLE owned_table OWNER TO test_owner1;

REVOKE ALL ON owned_table FROM PUBLIC;

-- Record initial pg_init_privs entry

WHERE objid = 'owned_table'::regclass;

-- Execution: Change ownership (should not affect pg_init_privs entries)

WHERE objid = 'owned_table'::regclass;

subject: pgsql: Ensure we allocate NAMEDATALEN bytes for names in Index Only Sca
author: David Rowley <drowley(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Ensure we allocate NAMEDATALEN bytes for names in Index Only Scans
As an optimization, we store "name" columns as cstrings in btreeindexes.
Here we modify it so that Index Only Scans convert these cstrings backto names with NAMEDATALEN bytes rather than storing the cstring in thetuple slot, as was happening previously.
Bug: #17855Reported-by: Alexander LakhinReviewed-by: Alexander Lakhin, Tom LaneDiscussion: https://postgr.es/m/17855-5f523e0f9769a566@postgresql.orgBackpatch-through: 12, all supported versions

before_code: elog(ERROR, "no data returned for index-only scan");
* right now we don't need it elsewhere.
* Note: we must use the tupdesc supplied by the AM in index_deform_tuple,
* build the index scan keys from the index qualification
* any ORDER BY exprs have to be turned into scankeys in the same way
after_code: elog(ERROR, "no data returned for index-only scan");
* right now we don't need it elsewhere.
* Note: we must use the tupdesc supplied by the AM in index_deform_tuple,
* Copy all name columns stored as cstrings back into a NAMEDATALEN byte
* sized allocation.  We mark this branch as unlikely as generally "name"
* is used only for the system catalogs and this would have to be a user
* query running on those or some other user table with an index on a name
/* allocate the NAMEDATALEN and copy the datum into that memory */
/* use namestrcpy to zero-pad all trailing bytes */
* build the index scan keys from the index qualification
* any ORDER BY exprs have to be turned into scankeys in the same way
* The "name" type for btree uses text_ops which results in storing
* cstrings in the indexed keys rather than names.  Here we detect that in
* a generic way in case other index AMs want to do the same optimization.
* Check for opclasses with an opcintype of NAMEOID and an index tuple
* descriptor with CSTRINGOID.  If any of these are found, create an array
* marking the index attribute number of each of them.  StoreIndexTuple()
* handles copying the name Datums into a NAMEDATALEN-byte allocation.
/* First, count the number of such index keys */
* Now create an array to mark the attribute numbers of the keys that
* need to be converted from cstring to name.

added: * Copy all name columns stored as cstrings back into a NAMEDATALEN byte
* sized allocation.  We mark this branch as unlikely as generally "name"
* is used only for the system catalogs and this would have to be a user
* query running on those or some other user table with an index on a name
/* allocate the NAMEDATALEN and copy the datum into that memory */
/* use namestrcpy to zero-pad all trailing bytes */
* The "name" type for btree uses text_ops which results in storing
* cstrings in the indexed keys rather than names.  Here we detect that in
* a generic way in case other index AMs want to do the same optimization.
* Check for opclasses with an opcintype of NAMEOID and an index tuple
* descriptor with CSTRINGOID.  If any of these are found, create an array
* marking the index attribute number of each of them.  StoreIndexTuple()
* handles copying the name Datums into a NAMEDATALEN-byte allocation.
/* First, count the number of such index keys */
* Now create an array to mark the attribute numbers of the keys that
* need to be converted from cstring to name.

subject: pgsql: Revert "Fix parallel-safety check of expressions and predicate f
author: Michael Paquier <michael(at)paquier(dot)xyz>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Revert "Fix parallel-safety check of expressions and predicate for index builds"
This reverts commit eae7be600be7, following a discussion with Tom Lane,due to concerns that this impacts the decisions made by the planner forthe number of workers spawned based on the inlining and const-folding ofindex expressions and predicate for cases that would have worked untilthis commit.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/162802.1709746091@sss.pgh.pa.usBackpatch-through: 12

before_code: * Currently, parallel workers can't access the leader's temporary tables.
* Furthermore, any index predicate or index expressions must be parallel
* Fetch the list of expressions and predicates directly from the
* catalogs.  Retrieving this information from the relcache would cause
* the expressions and predicates to be flattened, losing properties that
* can be important to check if parallel workers can be used.  For
* example, immutable parallel-unsafe functions, that cannot be used in
* parallel workers, would be changed to Const nodes, that are safe in
after_code: * Currently, parallel workers can't access the leader's temporary tables.
* Furthermore, any index predicate or index expressions must be parallel
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/optimizer/plan/planner.c b/src/backend/optimizer/plan/planner.c

index d1552fb5eab68519acede72a0f25746b271e30a9..443ab08d75ab57bfd8849d43112bf926fabfbb2b 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/optimizer/plan/planner.c
+++ b/src/backend/optimizer/plan/planner.c
@@ -6667,18 +6667,10 @@ plan_create_index_workers(Oid tableOid, Oid indexOid)
     * Currently, parallel workers can't access the leader's temporary tables.
     * Furthermore, any index predicate or index expressions must be parallel
     * safe.
-    *
-    * Fetch the list of expressions and predicates directly from the
-    * catalogs.  Retrieving this information from the relcache would cause
-    * the expressions and predicates to be flattened, losing properties that
-    * can be important to check if parallel workers can be used.  For
-    * example, immutable parallel-unsafe functions, that cannot be used in
-    * parallel workers, would be changed to Const nodes, that are safe in
-    * parallel workers.
     */
    if (heap->rd_rel->relpersistence == RELPERSISTENCE_TEMP ||
-       !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) get_index_expressions(indexOid)) ||
-       !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) get_index_predicate(indexOid)))
+       !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) RelationGetIndexExpressions(index)) ||
+       !is_parallel_safe(root, (Node *) RelationGetIndexPredicate(index)))
    {
        parallel_workers = 0;
        goto done;

removed: * Fetch the list of expressions and predicates directly from the
* catalogs.  Retrieving this information from the relcache would cause
* the expressions and predicates to be flattened, losing properties that
* can be important to check if parallel workers can be used.  For
* example, immutable parallel-unsafe functions, that cannot be used in
* parallel workers, would be changed to Const nodes, that are safe in

    -- Simulate parallel-unsafe operation, e.g., accessing a sequence

        <description>Test parallel index build with a parallel-unsafe volatile function in index expression, expecting no parallel workers.</description>

INSERT INTO test_volatile_expr SELECT i FROM generate_series(1, 10000) i;

    RETURN x + random()::INT;

        </sql>

subject: pgsql: Fix type-checking of RECORD-returning functions in FROM.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix type-checking of RECORD-returning functions in FROM.
In the corner case where a function returning RECORD has beensimplified to a RECORD constant or an inlined ROW() expression,ExecInitFunctionScan failed to cross-check the function's resultrowtype against the coldeflist provided by the calling query.That happened because get_expr_result_type is able to extract atupdesc from such expressions, which led ExecInitFunctionScan toignore the coldeflist.  (Instead, it used the extracted tupdescto check the function's output, which of course always succeeds.)
I have not been able to demonstrate any really serious consequencesfrom this, because if some column of the result is of the wrongtype and is directly referenced by a Var of the calling query,CheckVarSlotCompatibility will catch it.  However, we definitely dofail to report the case where the function returns more columns thanthe coldeflist expects, and in the converse case where it returnsfewer columns, we get an assert failure (but, seemingly, no worseresults in non-assert builds).
To fix, always build the expected tupdesc from the coldeflist if thereis one, and consult get_expr_result_type only when there isn't one.
Also remove the failing Assert, even though it is no longer reachedafter this fix.  It doesn't seem to be adding anything useful, sincelater checking will deal with cases with the wrong number of columns.
The only other place I could find that is doing something similaris inline_set_returning_function.  There's no live bug there becausewe cannot be looking at a Const or RowExpr, but for consistencychange that code to agree with ExecInitFunctionScan.
Per report from PetSerAl.  After some debate I've concluded thatthis should be back-patched.  There is a small risk that somebodyhas been relying on such a case not throwing an error, but I judgethis outweighed by the risk that I've missed some way in which thefailure to cross-check has worse consequences than sketched above.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAKygsHSerA1eXsJHR9wft3Gn3wfHQ5RfP8XHBzF70_qcrrRvEg@mail.gmail.com

before_code: * Now determine if the function returns a simple or composite type,
* and build an appropriate tupdesc.  Note that in the composite case,
* the function may now return more columns than it did when the plan
* was made; we have to ignore any columns beyond "colcount".
/* Composite data type, e.g. a table's row type */

subject: pgsql: Explicitly list dependent types as extension members in pg_depen
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Explicitly list dependent types as extension members in pg_depend.
Auto-generated array types, multirange types, and relation rowtypesare treated as dependent objects: they can't be dropped separatelyfrom the base object, nor can they have their own ownership orpermissions.  We previously felt that, for objects that are in anextension, only the base object needs to be listed as an extensionmember in pg_depend.  While that's sufficient to prevent inappropriatedrops, it results in undesirable answers if someone asks whether adependent type belongs to the extension.  It looks like the dependenttype is just some random separately-created object that happens todepend on the base object.  Notably, this results in postgres_fdwconcluding that expressions involving an array type are not shippableto the remote server, even when the defining extension has beenwhitelisted.
To fix, cause GenerateTypeDependencies to make extension dependenciesfor dependent types as well as their base objects, and adjustExecAlterExtensionContentsStmt so that object addition and removaloperations recurse to dependent types.  The latter change means thatpg_upgrade of a type-defining extension will end with the dependenttype(s) now also listed as extension members, even if they werenot that way in the source database.  Normally we want pg_upgradeto precisely reproduce the source extension's state, but it seemsdesirable to make an exception here.
This is arguably a bug fix, but we can't back-patch it since itcauses changes in the expected contents of pg_depend.  (Becauseit does, I've bumped catversion, even though there's no changein the immediate post-initdb catalog contents.)
Tom Lane and David Geier
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/4a847c55-489f-4e8d-a664-fc6b1cbe306f@gmail.com

before_code: * We make an extension-membership dependency if we're in an extension
* script and makeExtensionDep is true (and isDependentType isn't true).
* makeExtensionDep should be true when creating a new type or replacing a
* shell type, but not for ALTER TYPE on an existing type.  Passing false
* causes the type's extension membership to be left alone.
* Make dependencies on namespace, owner, ACL, extension.
* Skip these for a dependent type, since it will have such dependencies
* indirectly through its depended-on type or relation.  An exception is
/* Normal dependencies on the I/O and support functions */
after_code: * We make an extension-membership dependency if we're in an extension
* makeExtensionDep should be true when creating a new type or replacing a
* shell type, but not for ALTER TYPE on an existing type.  Passing false
* causes the type's extension membership to be left alone.
* Make dependencies on namespace, owner, ACL.
* Skip these for a dependent type, since it will have such dependencies
* indirectly through its depended-on type or relation.  An exception is
* We used to skip this for dependent types, but it seems better to record
* their extension membership explicitly; otherwise code such as
* postgres_fdw's shippability test will be fooled.
/* Normal dependencies on the I/O and support functions */

@@ -599,7 +599,7 @@ GenerateTypeDependencies(HeapTuple typeTuple,

-    * Make dependencies on namespace, owner, ACL, extension.

+    * their extension membership explicitly; otherwise code such as

+    */

+       recordDependencyOnCurrentExtension(&myself, rebuild);
+

CREATE SCHEMA test_schema;
CREATE EXTENSION test_ext;
CREATE TYPE test_schema.mytype AS (x int);

-- Manually remove extension dependency from array type (simulating old behavior)
DELETE FROM pg_depend
WHERE classid = 'pg_type'::regclass
  AND objid = (SELECT oid FROM pg_type WHERE typname = '_mytype' AND typnamespace = 'test_schema'::regnamespace)
  AND refclassid = 'pg_extension'::regclass
  AND deptype = 'e';

-- Execution: Re-add the type to extension (should now recurse to array type due to patch)
ALTER EXTENSION test_ext DROP TYPE test_schema.mytype;
ALTER EXTENSION test_ext ADD TYPE test_schema.mytype;

-- Verify array type is now an extension member
SELECT COUNT(*) = 2 AS both_types_in_extension
FROM pg_depend d
JOIN pg_extension e ON d.refobjid = e.oid
WHERE e.extname = 'test_ext'
  AND d.classid = 'pg_type'::regclass
  AND d.deptype = 'e'
  AND d.objid IN (
    SELECT oid FROM pg_type 
    WHERE typnamespace = 'test_schema'::regnamespace 
      AND typname IN ('mytype', '_mytype')
  );

-- Teardown
DROP EXTENSION test_ext CASCADE;
DROP SCHEMA test_schema CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>

subject: pgsql: Promote assertion about !ReindexIsProcessingIndex to runtime err
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Promote assertion about !ReindexIsProcessingIndex to runtime error.
When this assertion was installed (in commit d2f60a3ab), I thoughtit was only for catching server logic errors that caused accesses tocatalogs that were undergoing index rebuilds.  However, it will alsofire in case of a user-defined index expression that attempts toaccess its own table.  We occasionally see reports of people tryingto do that, and typically getting unintelligible low-level errorsas a result.  We can provide a more on-point message by making thisa regular runtime check.
While at it, adjust the similar error check insystable_beginscan_ordered to use the same message text.  That oneis (probably) not reachable without a coding bug, but we might aswell use a translatable message if we have one.
Per bug #18363 from Alexander Lakhin.  Back-patch to all supportedbranches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18363-e3598a5a572d0699@postgresql.org

before_code: /* REINDEX can probably be a hard error here ... */
elog(ERROR, "cannot do ordered scan on index \"%s\", because it is being reindexed",
/* ... but we only throw a warning about violating IgnoreSystemIndexes */
after_code: /* REINDEX can probably be a hard error here ... */
errmsg("cannot access index \"%s\" while it is being reindexed",
/* ... but we only throw a warning about violating IgnoreSystemIndexes */

removed: elog(ERROR, "cannot do ordered scan on index \"%s\", because it is being reindexed",
added: errmsg("cannot access index \"%s\" while it is being reindexed",

subject: pgsql: Remove race condition in pg_get_expr().
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Remove race condition in pg_get_expr().
Since its introduction, pg_get_expr() has intended to silentlyreturn NULL if called with an invalid relation OID, as can happenwhen scanning the catalogs concurrently with relation drops.However, there is a race condition: we check validity of the OIDat the start, but it could get dropped just afterward, leading tofailures.  This is the cause of some intermittent instability we'reseeing in a proposed new test case, and presumably it's a hazard inthe field as well.
We can fix this by AccessShareLock-ing the target relation for theduration of pg_get_expr().  Since we don't require any permissionson the target relation, this is semantically a bit undesirable.  Butit turns out that the set_relation_column_names() subroutine alreadytakes a transient AccessShareLock on that relation, and has done sincecommit 2ffa740be in 2012.  Given the lack of complaints about that, itseems like there should be no harm in holding the lock a bit longer.
Back-patch to all supported branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/31ddcc01-a71b-4e8c-9948-01d1c47293ca@eisentraut.org

pg_get_expr_worker(text *expr, Oid relid, const char *relname, int prettyFlags)

* we need to transiently open and lock the rel, to make sure it won't go

* so this isn't really introducing any new behavior.)

subject: pgsql: Fix wrong logic in TransactionIdInRecentPast()
author: Alexander Korotkov <akorotkov(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix wrong logic in TransactionIdInRecentPast()
The TransactionIdInRecentPast() should return false for all the transactionsolder than TransamVariables->oldestClogXid.  However, the function containsa bug in comparison FullTransactionId to TransactionID allowing fulltransactions between nextXid - 2^32 and oldestClogXid - 2^31.
This commit fixes TransactionIdInRecentPast() by turning the oldestClogXid intoFullTransactionId first, then performing the comparison.
Backpatch to all supported versions.
Reported-by: Egor ChindyaskinBug: 18212Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18212-547307f8adf57262%40postgresql.orgAuthor: Karina LitskevichReviewed-by: Kyotaro HoriguchiBackpatch-through: 12

before_code: * If the transaction ID has wrapped around, it's definitely too old to
* determine the commit status.  Otherwise, we can compare it to
* ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid to determine whether the relevant
* CLOG entry is guaranteed to still exist.
after_code: * If fxid is not older than ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid, the
* relevant CLOG entry is guaranteed to still exist.  Convert
* ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid into a FullTransactionId to compare
* it with fxid.  Determine the right epoch knowing that oldest_fxid
* shouldn't be more than 2^31 older than now_fullxid.

removed: * If the transaction ID has wrapped around, it's definitely too old to
* determine the commit status.  Otherwise, we can compare it to
* ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid to determine whether the relevant
* CLOG entry is guaranteed to still exist.
added: * If fxid is not older than ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid, the
* relevant CLOG entry is guaranteed to still exist.  Convert
* ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid into a FullTransactionId to compare
* it with fxid.  Determine the right epoch knowing that oldest_fxid
* shouldn't be more than 2^31 older than now_fullxid.

-- Execute a query that internally uses TransactionIdInRecentPast, like pg_xact_status

-- Attempt to check status of an invalid XID (0) which is far in the past
-- This should return NULL or 'aborted' depending on CLOG, but tests the boundary logic

-- Teardown

lines.code: * If fxid is not older than ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid, the
* relevant CLOG entry is guaranteed to still exist.  Convert
* ShmemVariableCache->oldestClogXid into a FullTransactionId to compare
* it with fxid.  Determine the right epoch knowing that oldest_fxid
* shouldn't be more than 2^31 older than now_fullxid.

subject: pgsql: Fix assertion if index is dropped during REFRESH CONCURRENTLY
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix assertion if index is dropped during REFRESH CONCURRENTLY
When assertions are disabled, the built SQL statement is invalid andyou get a "syntax error". So this isn't a serious problem, but let'savoid the assertion failure.
Backpatch to all supported versions.
Reviewed-by: Noah Misch

patches.function: refresh_by_match_merge(Oid matviewOid, Oid tempOid, Oid relowner,
before_code: * ExecRefreshMatView() checks that after taking the exclusive lock on the
* matview. So at least one unique index is guaranteed to exist here
* because the lock is still being held; so an Assert seems sufficient.
after_code: * ExecRefreshMatView() checks that after taking the exclusive lock on the
* matview. So at least one unique index is guaranteed to exist here
* because the lock is still being held.  (One known exception is if a
* function called as part of refreshing the matview drops the index.
* That's a pretty silly thing to do.)
elog(ERROR, "could not find suitable unique index on materialized view");
patches.raw_diff:
diff --git a/src/backend/commands/matview.c b/src/backend/commands/matview.c

index e87bdac28510c6b3218b67bb641b1eae02859138..d82bee57d92cf2a69db4f0a3b3110ced62f72ed8 100644 (file)

--- a/src/backend/commands/matview.c
+++ b/src/backend/commands/matview.c
@@ -795,9 +795,12 @@ refresh_by_match_merge(Oid matviewOid, Oid tempOid, Oid relowner,
     *
     * ExecRefreshMatView() checks that after taking the exclusive lock on the
     * matview. So at least one unique index is guaranteed to exist here
-    * because the lock is still being held; so an Assert seems sufficient.
+    * because the lock is still being held.  (One known exception is if a
+    * function called as part of refreshing the matview drops the index.
+    * That's a pretty silly thing to do.)
     */
-   Assert(foundUniqueIndex);
+   if (!foundUniqueIndex)
+       elog(ERROR, "could not find suitable unique index on materialized view");
 
    appendStringInfoString(&querybuf,
                           " AND newdata.* OPERATOR(pg_catalog.*=) mv.*) "

removed: * because the lock is still being held; so an Assert seems sufficient.
added: * because the lock is still being held.  (One known exception is if a
* function called as part of refreshing the matview drops the index.
* That's a pretty silly thing to do.)
elog(ERROR, "could not find suitable unique index on materialized view");

    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test REFRESH CONCURRENTLY when a unique index is dropped during refresh via a volatile function, expecting an ERROR.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_drop_index CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_drop CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS drop_index_func() CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_base_drop (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT);
INSERT INTO test_base_drop VALUES (1, 100);
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW test_mv_drop_index AS SELECT * FROM test_base_drop;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX test_mv_unique_idx ON test_mv_drop_index (id);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION drop_index_func()
RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    DROP INDEX test_mv_unique_idx;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql VOLATILE;

-- Execution: Attempt refresh, which internally may call the function.
-- This simulates the scenario where an index is dropped during refresh.
-- The exact trigger mechanism is complex; we use a simple direct call to simulate.
-- For a true test, the function would need to be invoked as part of the refresh.
-- This is a simplified representation.
BEGIN;
    REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY test_mv_drop_index;
    -- In a real scenario, the drop would happen concurrently.
    -- We directly call the function to simulate the drop.
    PERFORM drop_index_func();
ROLLBACK;

-- Teardown
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_drop_index CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_drop CASCADE;
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS drop_index_func() CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test REFRESH CONCURRENTLY on a materialized view without any unique index, expecting an ERROR.</description>
        <sql>

DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_no_unique CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_no_unique CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_base_no_unique (id INT, info TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_base_no_unique VALUES (1, 'x');
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW test_mv_no_unique AS SELECT * FROM test_base_no_unique;
-- Do not create any unique index.

-- Execution
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY test_mv_no_unique;

-- Teardown
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_no_unique CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_no_unique CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test REFRESH CONCURRENTLY with a unique constraint (implicitly creates a unique index) to verify it works.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_unique_constraint CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_constraint CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_base_constraint (a INT, b TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_base_constraint VALUES (10, 'foo'), (20, 'bar');
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW test_mv_unique_constraint AS SELECT * FROM test_base_constraint;
ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW test_mv_unique_constraint ADD UNIQUE (a);

-- Execution
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY test_mv_unique_constraint;

-- Teardown
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_unique_constraint CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_constraint CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test that after a failed REFRESH CONCURRENTLY due to missing unique index, the materialized view remains usable and can be refreshed after adding an index.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_recover CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_recover CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_base_recover (key INT, value TEXT);
INSERT INTO test_base_recover VALUES (1, 'initial');
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW test_mv_recover AS SELECT * FROM test_base_recover;
-- Initially no unique index.

-- Execution: First refresh should fail.
BEGIN;
    REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY test_mv_recover;
ROLLBACK;

-- Add a unique index.
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON test_mv_recover (key);

-- Refresh should now succeed.
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY test_mv_recover;

-- Verify data.
SELECT * FROM test_mv_recover ORDER BY key;

-- Teardown
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS test_mv_recover CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_base_recover CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * because the lock is still being held.  (One known exception is if a
* function called as part of refreshing the matview drops the index.
* That's a pretty silly thing to do.)

subject: pgsql: Apply band-aid fix for an oversight in reparameterize_path_by_ch
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Apply band-aid fix for an oversight in reparameterize_path_by_child.
The path we wish to reparameterize is not a standalone object:in particular, it implicitly references baserestrictinfo clausesin the associated RelOptInfo, and if it's a SampleScan path thenthere is also the TableSampleClause in the RTE to worry about.Both of those could contain lateral references to the join partnerrelation, which would need to be modified to refer to its child.Since we aren't doing that, affected queries can give wrong answers,or odd failures such as "variable not found in subplan target list",or executor crashes.  But we can't just summarily modify thoseexpressions, because they are shared with other paths for the rel.We'd break things if we modify them and then end up using somenon-partitioned-join path.
In HEAD, we plan to fix this by postponing reparameterizationuntil create_plan(), when we know that those other paths areno longer of interest, and then adjusting those expressions alongwith the ones in the path itself.  That seems like too big a changefor stable branches however.  In the back branches, let's just detectwhether any troublesome lateral references actually exist in thoseexpressions, and fail reparameterization if so.  This will result innot performing a partitioned join in such cases.  Given the lack offield complaints, nobody's likely to miss the optimization.
Report and patch by Richard Guo.  Apply to 12-16 only, sincethe intended fix for HEAD looks quite different.  We're not quiteready to push the HEAD fix, but with back-branch releases comingup soon, it seems wise to get this stopgap fix in place there.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAMbWs496+N=UAjOc=rcD3P7B6oJe4rZw08e_TZRUsWbPxZW3Tw@mail.gmail.com

* the other relation, we can't reparameterize, because we must
* not change the RelOptInfo's contents here.  (Doing so would
* break things if we end up using a non-partitionwise join.)
* If it's a SampleScan with tablesample parameters referencing
* the other relation, we can't reparameterize, because we must

-- Setup

CREATE TABLE part_tab_p1 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (100);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p2 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (100) TO (200);
CREATE TABLE other_tab (id INT, val INT);
INSERT INTO part_tab SELECT i, i*10 FROM generate_series(1,199) i;
INSERT INTO other_tab SELECT i, i*5 FROM generate_series(1,100) i;
ANALYZE part_tab, other_tab;

-- Execution: lateral reference in WHERE clause referencing other_tab
SELECT * FROM part_tab p JOIN other_tab o ON p.id = o.id WHERE p.val > (SELECT avg(val) FROM other_tab o2 WHERE o2.id = o.id);

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test SampleScan path with tablesample clause containing lateral reference to join partner, expecting reparameterization to fail.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE part_tab (id INT, val INT) PARTITION BY RANGE(id);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p1 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (100);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p2 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (100) TO (200);
CREATE TABLE other_tab (id INT, val INT);
INSERT INTO part_tab SELECT i, i*10 FROM generate_series(1,199) i;
INSERT INTO other_tab SELECT i, i*5 FROM generate_series(1,100) i;
ANALYZE part_tab, other_tab;

-- Execution: TABLESAMPLE with lateral reference to other_tab in REPEATABLE clause
SELECT * FROM part_tab TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM (10) REPEATABLE (other_tab.id) p JOIN other_tab ON p.id = other_tab.id;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test partitioned join with PlaceHolderVar lateral reference in baserestrictinfo, expecting reparameterization to fail.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE part_tab (id INT, val INT) PARTITION BY RANGE(id);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p1 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (100);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p2 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (100) TO (200);
CREATE TABLE other_tab (id INT, val INT);
INSERT INTO part_tab SELECT i, i*10 FROM generate_series(1,199) i;
INSERT INTO other_tab SELECT i, i*5 FROM generate_series(1,100) i;
ANALYZE part_tab, other_tab;

-- Execution: Use subquery to create PlaceHolderVar with lateral reference
SELECT * FROM part_tab p JOIN other_tab o ON p.id = o.id WHERE p.val > (SELECT sum(o2.val) FROM other_tab o2 WHERE o2.id = o.id);

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test partitioned join with IndexPath and lateral reference in baserestrictinfo, expecting reparameterization to fail for index scan.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE part_tab (id INT PRIMARY KEY, val INT) PARTITION BY RANGE(id);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p1 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (100);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p2 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (100) TO (200);
CREATE TABLE other_tab (id INT, val INT);
INSERT INTO part_tab SELECT i, i*10 FROM generate_series(1,199) i;
INSERT INTO other_tab SELECT i, i*5 FROM generate_series(1,100) i;
ANALYZE part_tab, other_tab;

-- Execution: Force index scan with lateral reference in WHERE
SET enable_seqscan = off;
SET enable_bitmapscan = off;
SELECT * FROM part_tab p JOIN other_tab o ON p.id = o.id WHERE p.val > (SELECT avg(val) FROM other_tab o2 WHERE o2.id = o.id);
RESET enable_seqscan;
RESET enable_bitmapscan;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test partitioned join without problematic lateral references, expecting successful reparameterization and partitioned join execution.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE part_tab (id INT, val INT) PARTITION BY RANGE(id);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p1 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (1) TO (100);
CREATE TABLE part_tab_p2 PARTITION OF part_tab FOR VALUES FROM (100) TO (200);
CREATE TABLE other_tab (id INT, val INT);
INSERT INTO part_tab SELECT i, i*10 FROM generate_series(1,199) i;
INSERT INTO other_tab SELECT i, i*5 FROM generate_series(1,100) i;
ANALYZE part_tab, other_tab;

-- Execution: Simple join without lateral references to other relation in baserestrictinfo
SELECT * FROM part_tab p JOIN other_tab o ON p.id = o.id WHERE p.val > 50;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS part_tab CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS other_tab CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Fix locking when fixing an incomplete split of a GIN internal pa
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix locking when fixing an incomplete split of a GIN internal page
ginFinishSplit() expects the caller to hold an exclusive lock on thebuffer, but when finishing an earlier "leftover" incomplete split ofan internal page, the caller held a shared lock. That caused anassertion failure in MarkBufferDirty(). Without assertions, it couldlead to corruption if two backends tried to complete the split at thesame time.
On master, add a test case using the new injection point facility.
Report and analysis by Fei Changhong. Backpatch the fix to allsupported versions.
Reviewed-by: Fei Changhong, Michael PaquierDiscussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/tencent_A3CE810F59132D8E230475A5F0F7A08C8307@qq.com

before_code: * Lock buffer by needed method for search.
* ok, page is correctly locked, we should check to move right ..,
if (GinPageIsLeaf(page))    /* we found, return locked page */
* The next page is locked first, before releasing the current page. This is
* crucial to protect from concurrent page deletion (see comment in
ginStepRight(Buffer buffer, Relation index, int lockmode)
* freestack == false when we encounter an incompletely split page during
* a scan, while freestack == true is used in the normal scenario that a
* split is finished right after the initial insert.
elog(DEBUG1, "finishing incomplete split of block %u in gin index \"%s\"",
/* this loop crawls up the stack until the insertion is complete */
* Insert a value to tree described by stack.
/* If the leaf page was incompletely split, finish the split first */
after_code: * Lock buffer by needed method for search.
* ok, page is correctly locked, we should check to move right ..,
if (GinPageIsLeaf(page))    /* we found, return locked page */
* The next page is locked first, before releasing the current page. This is
* crucial to prevent concurrent VACUUM from deleting a page that we are about
* to step to. (The lock-coupling isn't strictly necessary when we are
* traversing the tree to find an insert location, because page deletion grabs
* a cleanup lock on the root to prevent any concurrent inserts. See Page
* deletion section in the README. But there's no harm in doing it always.)
ginStepRight(Buffer buffer, Relation index, int lockmode)
/* this loop crawls up the stack until the insertion is complete */
* An entry point to ginFinishSplit() that is used when we stumble upon an
* existing incompletely split page in the tree, as opposed to completing a
* split that we just made outselves. The difference is that stack->buffer may
* be merely share-locked on entry, and will be upgraded to exclusive mode.
* Note: Upgrading the lock momentarily releases it. Doing that in a scan
* would not be OK, because a concurrent VACUUM might delete the page while
* we're not holding the lock. It's OK in an insert, though, because VACUUM
* has a different mechanism that prevents it from running concurrently with
* inserts. (Namely, it holds a cleanup lock on the root.)
ginFinishOldSplit(GinBtree btree, GinBtreeStack *stack, GinStatsData *buildStats, int access)
elog(DEBUG1, "finishing incomplete split of block %u in gin index \"%s\"",
* Someone else already completed the split while we were not
* Insert a value to tree described by stack.
/* If the leaf page was incompletely split, finish the split first */

removed: * crucial to protect from concurrent page deletion (see comment in
added: * crucial to prevent concurrent VACUUM from deleting a page that we are about
* to step to. (The lock-coupling isn't strictly necessary when we are
* traversing the tree to find an insert location, because page deletion grabs
* a cleanup lock on the root to prevent any concurrent inserts. See Page
* deletion section in the README. But there's no harm in doing it always.)

removed: * freestack == false when we encounter an incompletely split page during
* a scan, while freestack == true is used in the normal scenario that a
* split is finished right after the initial insert.
elog(DEBUG1, "finishing incomplete split of block %u in gin index \"%s\"",

added: * An entry point to ginFinishSplit() that is used when we stumble upon an
* existing incompletely split page in the tree, as opposed to completing a
* split that we just made outselves. The difference is that stack->buffer may
* be merely share-locked on entry, and will be upgraded to exclusive mode.
* Note: Upgrading the lock momentarily releases it. Doing that in a scan
* would not be OK, because a concurrent VACUUM might delete the page while
* we're not holding the lock. It's OK in an insert, though, because VACUUM
* has a different mechanism that prevents it from running concurrently with
* inserts. (Namely, it holds a cleanup lock on the root.)
ginFinishOldSplit(GinBtree btree, GinBtreeStack *stack, GinStatsData *buildStats, int access)
elog(DEBUG1, "finishing incomplete split of block %u in gin index \"%s\"",
* Someone else already completed the split while we were not

generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test concurrent insert operations that may encounter incomplete splits in GIN index, verifying lock upgrade from shared to exclusive works correctly.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_incomplete_split CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gin_incomplete_split (t text);
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS btree_gin;
CREATE INDEX test_gin_idx ON test_gin_incomplete_split USING gin (t gin_trgm_ops);
INSERT INTO test_gin_incomplete_split SELECT md5(random()::text) FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Execution: Concurrent inserts to trigger potential incomplete splits
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_gin_incomplete_split VALUES ('concurrent_test_value_1');
INSERT INTO test_gin_incomplete_split VALUES ('concurrent_test_value_2');
COMMIT;

-- Verify index integrity
SET enable_seqscan = off;
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF) SELECT * FROM test_gin_incomplete_split WHERE t LIKE '%test%';
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_gin_incomplete_split WHERE t LIKE '%test%';

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_incomplete_split CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test GIN index scan with incomplete split detection during search mode (shared lock), ensuring no assertion failures.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_scan_split CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gin_scan_split (data tsvector);
CREATE INDEX test_gin_scan_idx ON test_gin_scan_split USING gin (data);
INSERT INTO test_gin_scan_split SELECT to_tsvector('english', 'sample text ' || g) FROM generate_series(1, 500) g;

-- Execution: Force potential incomplete splits by creating index under load
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_gin_scan_split VALUES (to_tsvector('english', 'trigger split'));
COMMIT;

-- Perform scan that may encounter incomplete splits
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_gin_scan_split WHERE data @@ to_tsquery('english', 'trigger');

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_scan_split CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test GIN index insertion with multiple levels to verify recursive incomplete split handling in internal pages.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_multi_level CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gin_multi_level (arr int[]);
CREATE INDEX test_gin_multi_idx ON test_gin_multi_level USING gin (arr);
-- Insert enough data to create multi-level GIN index
INSERT INTO test_gin_multi_level SELECT ARRAY[g, g*2, g*3] FROM generate_series(1, 10000) g;

-- Execution: Insert values that may cause splits at different levels
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_gin_multi_level VALUES ('{99999, 199998, 299997}');
INSERT INTO test_gin_multi_level VALUES ('{99998, 199996, 299994}');
COMMIT;

-- Verify index can still be used
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_gin_multi_level WHERE arr @> '{99999}';

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_multi_level CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test GIN index with concurrent VACUUM operations to ensure lock coupling prevents page deletion issues during incomplete split completion.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_vacuum CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gin_vacuum (keywords text[]);
CREATE INDEX test_gin_vacuum_idx ON test_gin_vacuum USING gin (keywords);
INSERT INTO test_gin_vacuum SELECT ARRAY['key' || g, 'word' || g] FROM generate_series(1, 5000) g;

-- Execution: Perform VACUUM concurrently with inserts
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_gin_vacuum VALUES (ARRAY['concurrent', 'test']);
VACUUM (DISABLE_PAGE_SKIPPING) test_gin_vacuum;
COMMIT;

-- Verify index operations still work
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_gin_vacuum WHERE keywords @> ARRAY['concurrent'];

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_vacuum CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test GIN index with injection point simulation to trigger incomplete split scenario and verify lock upgrade logic.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup: Use pg_inject_fault if available (requires superuser and debug build)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_injection CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_gin_injection (doc tsvector);
CREATE INDEX test_gin_injection_idx ON test_gin_injection USING gin (doc);
INSERT INTO test_gin_injection SELECT to_tsvector('english', 'document ' || g) FROM generate_series(1, 1000) g;

-- Execution: Attempt to trigger incomplete split scenario
-- Note: Actual injection requires debug build and superuser privileges
-- This test simulates the condition by concurrent operations
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test_gin_injection VALUES (to_tsvector('english', 'incomplete split test'));
SAVEPOINT s1;
INSERT INTO test_gin_injection VALUES (to_tsvector('english', 'another test'));
ROLLBACK TO s1;
COMMIT;

-- Verify index integrity
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_gin_injection WHERE doc @@ to_tsquery('english', 'incomplete');

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_gin_injection CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

lines.code: * crucial to prevent concurrent VACUUM from deleting a page that we are about
* to step to. (The lock-coupling isn't strictly necessary when we are
* traversing the tree to find an insert location, because page deletion grabs
* a cleanup lock on the root to prevent any concurrent inserts. See Page
* deletion section in the README. But there's no harm in doing it always.)
ginFinishOldSplit(GinBtree btree, GinBtreeStack *stack, GinStatsData *buildStats, int access)

subject: pgsql: Second attempt at organizing jsonpath operators and methods
author: Peter Eisentraut <peter(at)eisentraut(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Second attempt at organizing jsonpath operators and methods
Second attempt at 283a95da923.  Since we can't reorder the enum valuesof JsonPathItemType, instead reorder the switch cases where they areused to generally follow the order of the enum values, for bettermaintainability.
generated_sql_tests:
<test_cases>
    <test_case id="1">
        <description>Test jsonpath printing with various operators to verify reordering of switch cases in printJsonPathItem function.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsonpath_print CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsonpath_print (data jsonb, path jsonpath);
INSERT INTO test_jsonpath_print VALUES 
('{"a": 1, "b": 2}', '$.a + $.b'),
('{"arr": [1,2,3]}', '$.arr[*] ? (@ > 1)'),
('{"x": "value"}', '$.x like_regex "val"'),
('{"key": "test"}', '$.key starts with "te"'),
('{"n": null}', '$.n.type()');

-- Execution: Use jsonb_path_query to trigger jsonpath printing internally
SELECT data, path, jsonb_path_query(data, path) AS result FROM test_jsonpath_print ORDER BY path;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsonpath_print CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="2">
        <description>Test jspOperationName function reordering by using jsonpath operators in expressions.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_operation CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsp_operation (data jsonb);
INSERT INTO test_jsp_operation VALUES 
('{"a": 10, "b": 5}'),
('{"arr": [1,2,3]}'),
('{"val": "string"}');

-- Execution: Test various jsonpath operators affected by jspOperationName reordering
SELECT 
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a + $.b') AS add_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a - $.b') AS sub_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a * $.b') AS mul_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a / $.b') AS div_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a % $.b') AS mod_op
FROM test_jsp_operation WHERE data @> '{"a": 10, "b": 5}';

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_operation CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="3">
        <description>Test jspInitByBuffer reordering by using jsonpath items with different types.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_init CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsp_init (data jsonb, path jsonpath);
INSERT INTO test_jsp_init VALUES 
('{"num": 123.45}', '$.num'),
('{"str": "text"}', '$.str'),
('{"bool": true}', '$.bool'),
('{"null": null}', '$.null'),
('{"key": "value"}', '$.key');

-- Execution: Trigger jspInitByBuffer for various jsonpath item types
SELECT data, path, jsonb_path_exists(data, path) AS exists FROM test_jsp_init ORDER BY path;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_init CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="4">
        <description>Test jspGetArg assertion reordering by using unary operators and functions.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_getarg CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsp_getarg (data jsonb);
INSERT INTO test_jsp_getarg VALUES 
('{"val": -5}'),
('{"ts": "2023-01-01"}'),
('{"arr": [1,2,3]}');

-- Execution: Test jsonpath expressions using jpiNot, jpiPlus, jpiMinus, jpiExists, jpiDatetime
SELECT 
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '!($.val < 0)') AS not_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '+$.val') AS plus_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '-$.val') AS minus_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, 'exists($.arr[*])') AS exists_op,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, 'datetime($.ts)') AS datetime_op
FROM test_jsp_getarg;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_getarg CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
    <test_case id="5">
        <description>Test jspGetNext assertion reordering by using complex jsonpath expressions with multiple operators.</description>
        <sql>
-- Setup
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_getnext CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE test_jsp_getnext (data jsonb);
INSERT INTO test_jsp_getnext VALUES 
('{"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}'),
('{"arr": [10,20,30], "key": "test"}'),
('{"x": 5, "y": null}');

-- Execution: Complex jsonpath combining various operators to trigger jspGetNext
SELECT 
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.a + $.b * $.c') AS arithmetic,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.arr[*] ? (@ > 15 && @ < 35)') AS filter_logic,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.key starts with "te" || $.key like_regex "t.st"') AS string_ops,
    jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.x.type() == "number" && $.y is unknown') AS type_check
FROM test_jsp_getnext;

-- Teardown
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test_jsp_getnext CASCADE;
        </sql>
    </test_case>
</test_cases>

subject: pgsql: Check collation when creating partitioned index
author: Peter Eisentraut <peter(at)eisentraut(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Check collation when creating partitioned index
When creating a partitioned index, the partition key must be a subsetof the index's columns.  But this currently doesn't check that thecollations between the partition key and the index definition match.So you can construct a unique index that fails to enforce uniqueness.(This would most likely involve a nondeterministic collation, so itwould have to be crafted explicitly and is not something that wouldjust happen by accident.)
This patch adds the required collation check.  As a result, anypreviously allowed unique index that has a collation mismatch would nolonger be allowed to be created.
Reviewed-by: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Discussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/flat/3327cb54-f7f1-413b-8fdb-7a9dceebb938%40eisentraut.org

before_code: /* Matched the column, now what about the equality op? */
after_code: /* Matched the column, now what about the collation and equality op? */

removed: /* Matched the column, now what about the equality op? */
added: /* Matched the column, now what about the collation and equality op? */

subject: pgsql: Fix assertions with RI triggers in heap_update and heap_delete.
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix assertions with RI triggers in heap_update and heap_delete.
If the tuple being updated is not visible to the crosscheck snapshot,we return TM_Updated but the assertions would not hold in that case.Move them to before the cross-check.
Fixes bug #17893. Backpatch to all supported versions.
Author: Alexander LakhinBackpatch-through: 12Discussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/17893-35847009eec517b5%40postgresql.org

subject: pgsql: Guard against overflow in interval_mul() and interval_div().
author: Dean Rasheed <dean(dot)a(dot)rasheed(at)gmail(dot)com>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Guard against overflow in interval_mul() and interval_div().
Commits 146604ec43 and a898b409f6 added overflow checks tointerval_mul(), but not to interval_div(), which contains almostidentical code, and so is susceptible to the same kinds ofoverflows. In addition, those checks did not catch all possibleoverflow conditions.
Add additional checks to the "cascade down" code in interval_mul(),and copy all the overflow checks over to the corresponding code ininterval_div(), so that they both generate "interval out of range"errors, rather than returning bogus results.
Given that these errors are relatively easy to hit, back-patch to allsupported branches.
Per bug #18200 from Alexander Lakhin, and subsequent investigation.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18200-5ea288c7b2d504b1%40postgresql.org

before_code: * interval type has nothing equivalent to NaN.
* by the regular division code, causing all fields to be set to zero.
* Fractional months full days into days.  See comment in interval_mul().
after_code: * interval type has nothing equivalent to NaN.
* by the regular division code, causing all fields to be set to zero.
* Fractional months full days into days.  See comment in interval_mul().

subject: pgsql: Don't release index root page pin in ginFindParents().
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Don't release index root page pin in ginFindParents().
It's clearly stated in the comments that ginFindParents() must keepthe pin on the index's root page that's associated with the topmostGinBtreeStack item.  However, the code path for the case that thedesired downlink has been pushed down to the next index levelignored this proviso, and would release the pin anyway if we werestill examining the root level.  That led to an assertion failureor "buffer NNNN is not owned by resource owner" error later, whenwe try to release the pin again at the end of the insertion.
This is quite hard to reproduce, since it can only happen if anindex root page split occurs concurrently with our own insertion.Thanks to Jeff Janes for finding a test case that triggers itoften enough to allow investigation.
This has been there since the beginning of GIN, so back-patchto all supported branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAMkU=1yCAKtv86dMrD__Ja-7KzjE=uMeKX8y__cx5W-OEWy2ow@mail.gmail.com

after_code: /* Link not present in this level */
/* Do not release pin on the root buffer */

added: /* Link not present in this level */
/* Do not release pin on the root buffer */

subject: pgsql: Fix computation of varnullingrels when const-folding field selec
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix computation of varnullingrels when const-folding field selection.
We can simplify FieldSelect on a whole-row Var into a plain Varfor the selected field.  However, we should copy the whole-row Var'svarnullingrels when we do so, because the new Var is clearly nullableby exactly the same rels as the original.  Failure to do this led toerrors like "wrong varnullingrels (b) (expected (b 3)) for Var 2/2".
Richard Guo, per bug #18184 from Marian Krucina.  Back-patch tov16 where varnullingrels was introduced.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18184-5868dd258782058e@postgresql.org

after_code: /* New Var is nullable by same rels as the old one */

added: /* New Var is nullable by same rels as the old one */

subject: pgsql: Compute aggregate argument types correctly in transformAggregate
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Compute aggregate argument types correctly in transformAggregateCall().
transformAggregateCall() captures the datatypes of the aggregate'sarguments immediately to construct the Aggref.aggargtypes list.This seems reasonable because the arguments have already beentransformed --- but there is an edge case where they haven't been.Specifically, if we have an unknown-type literal in an ANY argumentposition, nothing will have been done with it earlier.  But if wealso have DISTINCT, then addTargetToGroupList() converts the literalto "text" type, resulting in the aggargtypes list not matching theactual runtime type of the argument.  The end result is that theaggregate tries to interpret a "text" value as being of type"unknown", that is a zero-terminated C string.  If the text valuecontains no zero bytes, this could result in disclosure of servermemory following the text literal value.
To fix, move the collection of the aggargtypes list to the endof transformAggregateCall(), after DISTINCT has been handled.This requires slightly more code, but not a great deal.
Our thanks to Jingzhou Fu for reporting this problem.
Security: CVE-2023-5868

before_code: * Before separating the args into direct and aggregated args, make a list
* of their data type OIDs for use later.
after_code: * Now build the aggargtypes list with the type OIDs of the direct and
* aggregated args, ignoring any resjunk entries that might have been
* added by ORDER BY/DISTINCT processing.  We can't do this earlier
* because said processing can modify some args' data types, in particular

removed: * Before separating the args into direct and aggregated args, make a list
* of their data type OIDs for use later.

added: * Now build the aggargtypes list with the type OIDs of the direct and
* aggregated args, ignoring any resjunk entries that might have been
* added by ORDER BY/DISTINCT processing.  We can't do this earlier
* because said processing can modify some args' data types, in particular

subject: pgsql: Detect integer overflow while computing new array dimensions.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Detect integer overflow while computing new array dimensions.
array_set_element() and related functions allow an array to beenlarged by assigning to subscripts outside the current array bounds.While these places were careful to check that the new bounds areallowable, they neglected to consider the risk of integer overflowin computing the new bounds.  In edge cases, we could compute newbounds that are invalid but get past the subsequent checks,allowing bad things to happen.  Memory stomps that are potentiallyexploitable for arbitrary code execution are possible, and so isdisclosure of server memory.
To fix, perform the hazardous computations using overflow-detectingarithmetic routines, which fortunately exist in all still-supportedbranches.
The test cases added for this generate (after patching) errors thatmention the value of MaxArraySize, which is platform-dependent.Rather than introduce multiple expected-files, use psql's VERBOSITYparameter to suppress the printing of the message text.  v11 psqllacks that parameter, so omit the tests in that branch.
Our thanks to Pedro Gallegos for reporting this problem.
Security: CVE-2023-5869

subject: pgsql: Dodge a compiler bug affecting timetz_zone/timetz_izone.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Dodge a compiler bug affecting timetz_zone/timetz_izone.
Use a modulo operator instead of implementing the same behaviorwith a loop.  The loop solution is doubtless microscopicallyfaster for the typical case of only wrapping into the very nextday, but maybe not so much for large interval values.  In anycase, timetz is such a backwater that it's doubtful anybodywould notice any performance change anyway.
This avoids a compiler bug occurring in AIX's xlc, even in prettylate-model revisions.
We did not have test coverage for the case where the initialresult->time value is negative, so add that.
For the moment, install this only in HEAD.  My plan is toback-patch the test case, and then the code change assuming thatbuildfarm testing proves the bug occurs in the back branches.(That seems pretty likely, but let's find out for sure.)
Per buildfarm results from commits 97957fdba and 2f0472030.Thanks to Michael Paquier for the idea to use a modulo operationto replace the faulty loop.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CA+hUKGK=DOC+hE-62FKfZy=Ybt5uLkrg3zCZD-jFykM-iPn8yw@mail.gmail.com

subject: pgsql: Fix briefly showing old progress stats for ANALYZE on inherited
author: Heikki Linnakangas <heikki(dot)linnakangas(at)iki(dot)fi>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix briefly showing old progress stats for ANALYZE on inherited tables.
ANALYZE on a table with inheritance children analyzes all the childtables in a loop. When stepping to next child table, it updated thechild rel ID value in the command progress stats, but did not resetthe 'sample_blks_total' and 'sample_blks_scanned' counters.acquire_sample_rows() updates 'sample_blks_total' as soon as the scanstarts and 'sample_blks_scanned' after processing the first block, butuntil then, pg_stat_progress_analyze would display a bogus combinationof the new child table relid with old counter values from thepreviously processed child table. Fix by resetting 'sample_blks_total'and 'sample_blks_scanned' to zero at the same time that'current_child_table_relid' is updated.
Backpatch to v13, where pg_stat_progress_analyze view was introduced.
Reported-by: Justin PryzbyDiscussion: https://www.postgresql.org/message-id/20230122162345.GP13860%40telsasoft.com

after_code: * Report progress.  The sampling function will normally report blocks
* done/total, but we need to reset them to 0 here, so that they don't
* show an old value until that.

added: * Report progress.  The sampling function will normally report blocks
* done/total, but we need to reset them to 0 here, so that they don't
* show an old value until that.

subject: pgsql: Fix edge-case for xl_tot_len broken by bae868ca.
author: Thomas Munro <tmunro(at)postgresql(dot)org>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Fix edge-case for xl_tot_len broken by bae868ca.
bae868ca removed a check that was still needed.  If you had anxl_tot_len at the end of a page that was too small for a record header,but not big enough to span onto the next page, we'd immediately performthe CRC check using a bogus large length.  Because of arbitrary codingdifferences between the CRC implementations on different platforms,nothing very bad happened on common modern systems.  On systems usingthe _sb8.c fallback we could segfault.
Restore that check, add a new assertion and supply a test for that case.Back-patch to 12, like bae868ca.
Tested-by: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>Tested-by: Alexander Lakhin <exclusion(at)gmail(dot)com>Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CA%2BhUKGLCkTT7zYjzOxuLGahBdQ%3DMcF%3Dz5ZvrjSOnW4EDhVjT-g%40mail.gmail.com

before_code: /* We'll validate the header once we have the next page. */
after_code: /* There may be no next page if it's too small. */
"invalid record length at %X/%X: expected at least %u, got %u",
/* We'll validate the header once we have the next page. */

added: /* There may be no next page if it's too small. */
"invalid record length at %X/%X: expected at least %u, got %u",

subject: pgsql: Limit to_tsvector_byid's initial array allocation to something s
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Limit to_tsvector_byid's initial array allocation to something sane.
The initial estimate of the number of distinct ParsedWords is justthat: an estimate.  Don't let it exceed what palloc is willing toallocate.  If in fact we need more entries, we'll eventually failtrying to enlarge the array.  But if we don't, this allows success oninputs that currently draw "invalid memory alloc request size".
Per bug #18080 from Uwe Binder.  Back-patch to all supported branches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18080-d5c5e58fef8c99b7@postgresql.org

subject: pgsql: Collect dependency information for parsed CallStmts.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Collect dependency information for parsed CallStmts.
Parse analysis of a CallStmt will inject mutable information,for instance the OID of the called procedure, so that subsequentDDL may create a need to re-parse the CALL.  We failed to detectthis for CALLs in plpgsql routines, because no dependency informationwas collected when putting a CallStmt into the plan cache.  Thatcould lead to misbehavior or strange errors such as "cache lookupfailed".
Before commit ee895a655, the issue would only manifest for CALLsappearing in atomic contexts, because we re-planned non-atomicCALLs every time through anyway.
It is now apparent that extract_query_dependencies() probablyneeds a special case for every utility statement type for whichstmt_requires_parse_analysis() returns true.  I wanted to addsomething like Assert(!stmt_requires_parse_analysis(...)) whenfalling out of extract_query_dependencies_walker without doinganything, but there are API issues as well as a more fundamentalpoint: stmt_requires_parse_analysis is supposed to be applied toraw parser output, so it'd be cheating to assume it will give thecorrect answer for post-parse-analysis trees.  I contented myselfwith adding a comment.
Per bug #18131 from Christian Stork.  Back-patch to all supportedbranches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18131-576854e79c5cd264@postgresql.org

after_code: * This logic must handle any utility command for which parse
* Notably, CALL requires its own processing.
/* We need not examine funccall, just the transformed exprs */
* Ignore other utility statements, except those (such as EXPLAIN)
* that contain a parsed-but-not-planned query.  For those, we
* just need to transfer our attention to the contained query.

subject: pgsql: Track nesting depth correctly when drilling down into RECORD Var
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Track nesting depth correctly when drilling down into RECORD Vars.
expandRecordVariable() failed to adjust the parse nesting structurecorrectly when recursing to inspect an outer-level Var.  This couldresult in assertion failures or core dumps in corner cases.
Likewise, get_name_for_var_field() failed to adjust the deparsenamespace stack correctly when recursing to inspect an outer-levelVar.  In this case the likely result was a "bogus varno" errorwhile deparsing a view.
Per bug #18077 from Jingzhou Fu.  Back-patch to all supportedbranches.
Richard Guo, with some adjustments by me
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18077-b9db97c6e0ab45d8@postgresql.org

subject: pgsql: Fix get_expr_result_type() to find field names for RECORD Consts

subject: pgsql: Allow extracting fields from a ROW() expression in more cases.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Allow extracting fields from a ROW() expression in more cases.
Teach get_expr_result_type() to manufacture a tuple descriptor directlyfrom a RowExpr node.  If the RowExpr has type RECORD, this is the onlyway to get a tupdesc for its result, since even if the rowtype has beenblessed, we don't have its typmod available at this point.  (If theRowExpr has some named composite type, we continue to let the existingcode handle it, since the RowExpr might well not have the correct columnnames embedded in it.)
This fixes assorted corner cases illustrated by the added regressiontests.
This is a back-patch of the v13-era commit 8b7a0f1d1 into previousbranches.  At the time I'd judged it not important enough to back-patch,but the upcoming fix for bug #18077 includes a test case that dependson this working correctly; and 8b7a0f1d1 has now aged long enough tohave good confidence that it won't break anything.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/10872.1572202006@sss.pgh.pa.usDiscussion: https://postgr.es/m/3607145.1694803130@sss.pgh.pa.us

before_code: /* handle as a generic expression; no chance to resolve RECORD */
after_code: /* We can resolve the record type by generating the tupdesc directly */
/* handle as a generic expression; no chance to resolve RECORD */

added: /* We can resolve the record type by generating the tupdesc directly */

subject: pgsql: Avoid unnecessary plancache revalidation of utility statements.
author: Tom Lane <tgl(at)sss(dot)pgh(dot)pa(dot)us>
email_body:
pgsql-committers



Avoid unnecessary plancache revalidation of utility statements.
Revalidation of a plancache entry (after a cache invalidation event)requires acquiring a snapshot.  Normally that is harmless, but notif the cached statement is one that needs to run without acquiring asnapshot.  We were already aware of that for TransactionStmts,but for some reason hadn't extrapolated to the other statements thatPlannedStmtRequiresSnapshot() knows mustn't set a snapshot.  This canlead to unexpected failures of commands such as SET TRANSACTIONISOLATION LEVEL.  We can fix it in the same way, by excluding thosecommand types from revalidation.
However, we can do even better than that: there is no need torevalidate for any statement type for which parse analysis, rewrite,and plan steps do nothing interesting, which is nearly all utilitycommands.  To mechanize this, invent a parser functionstmt_requires_parse_analysis() that tells whether parse analysis doesanything beyond wrapping a CMD_UTILITY Query around the raw parsetree.  If that's what it does, then rewrite and plan will justskip the Query, so that it is not possible for the same raw parsetree to produce a different plan tree after cache invalidation.
stmt_requires_parse_analysis() is basically equivalent to theexisting function analyze_requires_snapshot(), except that forobscure reasons that function omits ReturnStmt and CallStmt.It is unclear whether those were oversights or intentional.I have not been able to demonstrate a bug from not acquiring asnapshot while analyzing these commands, but at best it seems mightyfragile.  It seems safer to acquire a snapshot for parse analysis ofthese commands too, which allows making stmt_requires_parse_analysisand analyze_requires_snapshot equivalent.
In passing this fixes a second bug, which is that ResetPlanCachewould exclude ReturnStmts and CallStmts from revalidation.That's surely *not* safe, since they contain parsable expressions.
Per bug #18059 from Pavel Kulakov.  Back-patch to all supportedbranches.
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/18059-79c692f036b25346@postgresql.org

before_code: *     Returns true if a snapshot must be set before doing parse analysis
*     on the given raw parse tree.
/* yes, because we must analyze the contained statement */
/* other utility statements don't have any real parse analysis */
after_code: * Caution: when changing the set of statement types that have non-default
* processing here, see also stmt_requires_parse_analysis() and
*     Returns true if parse analysis will do anything non-trivial
*     with the given raw parse tree.
* Generally, this should return true for any statement type for which
* transformStmt() does more than wrap a CMD_UTILITY Query around it.
* When it returns false, the caller can assume that there is no situation
* in which parse analysis of the raw statement could need to be re-done.
* Currently, since the rewriter and planner do nothing for CMD_UTILITY
* Queries, a false result means that the entire parse analysis/rewrite/plan
* pipeline will never need to be re-done.  If that ever changes, callers
/* all other statements just get wrapped in a CMD_UTILITY Query */
*     Returns true if a snapshot must be set before doing parse analysis
*     on the given raw parse tree.
* Currently, this should return true in exactly the same cases that
* stmt_requires_parse_analysis() does, so we just invoke that function
* rather than duplicating it.  We keep the two entry points separate for
* clarity of callers, since from the callers' standpoint these are
* While there may someday be a statement type for which transformStmt()
* does something nontrivial and yet no snapshot is needed for that
* processing, it seems likely that making such a choice would be fragile.
* If you want to install an exception, document the reasoning for it in a

+    * Caution: when changing the set of statement types that have non-default
+    * processing here, see also stmt_requires_parse_analysis() and
+    * analyze_requires_snapshot().
+    */
    switch (nodeTag(parseTree))
    {
            /*
@@ -421,14 +426,22 @@ transformStmt(ParseState *pstate, Node *parseTree)
 }

- * analyze_requires_snapshot
- *     Returns true if a snapshot must be set before doing parse analysis
- *     on the given raw parse tree.
+ * stmt_requires_parse_analysis
+ *     Returns true if parse analysis will do anything non-trivial
+ *     with the given raw parse tree.
+ *
+ * Generally, this should return true for any statement type for which
+ * transformStmt() does more than wrap a CMD_UTILITY Query around it.
+ * When it returns false, the caller can assume that there is no situation
+ * in which parse analysis of the raw statement could need to be re-done.
  *
- * Classification here should match transformStmt().
+ * Currently, since the rewriter and planner do nothing for CMD_UTILITY
+ * Queries, a false result means that the entire parse analysis/rewrite/plan
+ * pipeline will never need to be re-done.  If that ever changes, callers
+ * will likely need adjustment.
  */
 bool
-analyze_requires_snapshot(RawStmt *parseTree)
+stmt_requires_parse_analysis(RawStmt *parseTree)
 {
    bool        result;
 
@@ -442,6 +455,7 @@ analyze_requires_snapshot(RawStmt *parseTree)
        case T_UpdateStmt:
        case T_MergeStmt:
        case T_SelectStmt:
+       case T_ReturnStmt:
        case T_PLAssignStmt:
            result = true;
            break;
@@ -452,12 +466,12 @@ analyze_requires_snapshot(RawStmt *parseTree)
        case T_DeclareCursorStmt:
        case T_ExplainStmt:
        case T_CreateTableAsStmt:
-           /* yes, because we must analyze the contained statement */
+       case T_CallStmt:
            result = true;
            break;
 
        default:
-           /* other utility statements don't have any real parse analysis */
+           /* all other statements just get wrapped in a CMD_UTILITY Query */
            result = false;
            break;
    }
@@ -465,6 +479,30 @@ analyze_requires_snapshot(RawStmt *parseTree)
    return result;
 }
 
+/*
+ * analyze_requires_snapshot
+ *     Returns true if a snapshot must be set before doing parse analysis
+ *     on the given raw parse tree.
+ */
+bool
+analyze_requires_snapshot(RawStmt *parseTree)
+{
+   /*
+    * Currently, this should return true in exactly the same cases that
+    * stmt_requires_parse_analysis() does, so we just invoke that function
+    * rather than duplicating it.  We keep the two entry points separate for
+    * clarity of callers, since from the callers' standpoint these are
+    * different conditions.
+    *
+    * While there may someday be a statement type for which transformStmt()
+    * does something nontrivial and yet no snapshot is needed for that
+    * processing, it seems likely that making such a choice would be fragile.
+    * If you want to install an exception, document the reasoning for it in a
+    * comment.
+    */
+   return stmt_requires_parse_analysis(parseTree);
+}
+
 /*
  * transformDeleteStmt -
  *   transforms a Delete Statement
